# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any four key features of ARM design philosophy.**

**Model Answer (12 lines max):**

1. **Load-Store Architecture:** Memory access is restricted to `LDR` and `STR`. All arithmetic and logical operations occur between registers only, enabling single-cycle execution.
2. **Large Uniform Register File:** 16 × 32-bit general-purpose registers (`R0`–`R15`) are visible to all instructions, reducing stack traffic.
3. **Fixed Instruction Width:** All ARM instructions are 32 bits wide, simplifying instruction fetch and pipelining.
4. **Conditional Execution:** A 4-bit condition field in every instruction (bits 28–31) allows nearly every instruction to be executed conditionally, eliminating most short branches and saving power.

*(Any 4 of the 7 pillars accepted. Marks: 1 mark per feature + ½ mark for each explanation.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between ARM and Thumb instruction sets.**

**Model Answer (tabular form preferred):**

| Parameter | ARM Mode | Thumb Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction width | 32 bits | 16 bits |
| Code density | Lower | ~65% of ARM (smaller) |
| Performance | Higher (full register access) | Slightly lower (limited ops) |
| Conditional execution | All 16 conditions | Most conditions (subset)) |
| Barrel shifter | Full access in operand-2 | Limited subset |
| Typical use | Performance loops, ISRs | Size-critical firmware, cold-boot code |
| Switch mechanism | `BX`/`BLX` instructions | Same — sets `T` bit in CPSR |

*(1 mark for each major differentiator × 3. Total 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **RISC design philosophy** adopted by ARM processors. Discuss how each of the following supports this philosophy: (i) Load-store architecture, (ii) Fixed instruction width, (iii) Large register file, (iv) Conditional execution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction to RISC philosophy:**
The RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) philosophy, pioneered by Patterson and Hennessy in the 1980s, holds that a small, highly optimized set of instructions, executed through deep pipelining and hardwired control, delivers better performance and lower power than a complex, microcoded CISC design. ARM is a **canonical implementation** of this philosophy.

**(i) Load-Store Architecture (1.5 marks):**
In ARM, only two instruction classes — `LDR` (load register) and `STR` (store register) — access memory. Every arithmetic/logic instruction reads from and writes to **registers only**. This **decouples the memory subsystem from the ALU**, allowing the data-processing unit to be optimized for register-register operations in a single cycle. The benefit: **predictable CPI ≈ 1** and trivial pipelining.

**(ii) Fixed Instruction Width (1.5 marks):**
Every ARM instruction is exactly **32 bits wide**. This means:
- The fetch unit can read exactly one word per cycle, regardless of instruction type.
- The decoder can begin parsing at a fixed offset within the cycle.
- The next PC is always `PC + 4`, eliminating variable-length prefix handling.
- It enables **deep pipelines** (ARM Cortex-A77 has 13 stages) with balanced stage delays.

**(iii) Large Register File (2 marks):**
ARM provides **16 user-visible 32-bit registers** (`R0`–`R12` are general-purpose, `R13`/`R14`/`R15` are SP/LR/PC). This large register set:
- **Reduces memory spills** during compilation (fewer variables pushed to stack).
- **Minimises procedure-call overhead** (caller/callee saving needs fewer registers).
- **Enables high IPC** by giving the out-of-order engine more values to track.
- Fits encoding-wise: 16 = 2⁴, so each register specifier is exactly 4 bits, leaving room for shift/operand fields.

**(iv) Conditional Execution (2 marks):**
The upper 4 bits of every instruction form a **condition field**. The instruction is executed only if the `CPSR` flags satisfy the condition (e.g., `EQ` requires Z=1). This is implemented by gating the write-back stage. The benefits:
- Replaces a **branch + branch-target instruction** with a single conditionally-executed instruction.
- Saves a fetch cycle and a branch-predictor entry.
- **Reduces dynamic power** by avoiding pipeline flushes.
- Critical for tight inner loops (e.g., `SUBS R0, R0, #1; BNE loop` becomes a single `SUBNES R0, R0, #1`).

**Conclusion:**
Each of these four features is not an isolated design choice; they are **synergistic**. Load-store gives uniform 1-cycle execution; fixed width gives pipelining; large register file gives compiler room; conditional execution eliminates branches. Together they yield the legendary **performance-per-watt** of ARM cores.

---

**(b)** A given ARM7TDMI processor runs at **60 MHz** with an average CPI of **1.2** for a benchmark of **10 million instructions**. Compute (i) execution time, (ii) MIPS rating, and (iii) effective cycles per instruction if the pipeline achieves 30% branch-penalty-free execution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Clock frequency $f_{\text{clk}} = 60$ MHz ⇒ $T_{\text{clk}} = 1/60\,\mu s \approx 16.67$ ns
- Average CPI = 1.2
- Instruction count $N = 10 \times 10^6$

**(i) Execution time (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{CPU}} &= N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}} \\
&= 10 \times 10^6 \times 1.2 \times \frac{1}{60 \times 10^6} \\
&= \frac{12 \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^6} \\
&= 0.20 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{CPU}} = 200 \text{ ms}}
$$

**(ii) MIPS rating (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MIPS} &= \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 10^6}{1.2 \times 10^6} \\
&= 50 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MIPS} = 50}
$$

**(iii) Effective CPI with conditional execution benefit (3 marks):**

If **30% of instructions are short branches** that are converted to conditional instructions (each saving 1 cycle of branch penalty), then:

Let original CPI = 1.2. The 30% branch instructions have an effective CPI reduction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{saved}} &= 0.30 \times 1.2 \times 1 \text{ cycle saved} = 0.36 \\
\text{CPI}_{\text{new}} &= 1.2 - 0.36 = 0.84
\end{aligned}
$$

But CPI cannot drop below 1.0 in a single-issue in-order pipeline; so realistically (with proper pipelining):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} &= 1.0 \times (1 - 0.30) + 0.84 \times 0.30 \\
&= 0.70 + 0.252 = 0.952
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} \approx 0.95}
$$

**New execution time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{new}} &= 10 \times 10^6 \times 0.95 \times \frac{1}{60 \times 10^6} \\
&= 0.1583 \text{ s} \\
&\approx 158 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup:**

$$
S = \frac{200}{158} = 1.266 \text{ (≈ 26.6% faster)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ARM processor core architecture** and describe how it embodies the RISC design philosophy. List any **four advantages** of RISC over CISC in embedded applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (described textually — draw in exam):**
The ARM core consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Register Bank:** 16 × 32-bit user registers + CPSR/SPSR.
2. **Barrel Shifter:** Performs shifts/rotates on Operand-2 in 0 cycles.
3. **ALU:** Performs arithmetic (ADD, SUB, CMP) and logic (AND, ORR, EOR) operations, updates CPSR flags.
4. **Address Register / Incrementer:** Generates addresses for PC-relative fetches and load/store.
5. **Instruction Decoder & Control:** Hardwired decode of 32-bit instructions.
6. **Memory Interface:** Separate read/write buses for instruction and data (modified Harvard).
7. **Write-Back Path:** Returns ALU results to register bank (conditional gate).

**RISC Embodiment in the Architecture (3 marks):**
- The **register bank is centralised** — every instruction reads from / writes to it; this matches the RISC principle of register-register operations.
- The **barrel shifter is on the data path** (not in ALU), so shifts are free — a RISC optimisation.
- The **decoder is small and hardwired** (not microcoded) — keeps transistor count low → low power.
- The **write-back stage is gated by condition codes** — supports conditional execution natively.

**Four Advantages of RISC over CISC in Embedded Systems (4 marks):**

1. **Lower power dissipation:** Fewer transistors and simpler decode ⇒ lower dynamic power $P = \alpha C V^2 f$. Critical for battery-powered IoT/phones.
2. **Deterministic real-time behaviour:** Single-cycle execution of most instructions makes interrupt latency predictable — vital for hard-real-time control.
3. **Smaller silicon area / lower cost:** Hardwired control uses 1/3 to 1/5 the transistors of a CISC microcode engine, reducing per-chip cost.
4. **Easier pipelining & higher clock rates:** Fixed instruction width allows deeper, balanced pipelines; modern ARM Cortex-A cores reach >3 GHz.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **role of conditional execution and the barrel shifter** in reducing instruction count and improving code density on ARM. Provide **one assembly example** for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Conditional Execution (3 marks):**
In conventional ISAs, a compare followed by a small action requires a branch:

```asm
CMP   R0, #0          ; 1 instruction, 1 cycle
BEQ   skip            ; 1 branch instruction, may flush pipeline
ADD   R1, R1, #1      ; executes only if Z=1
skip:
```

ARM compresses this into a single conditionally-executed instruction:

```asm
ADDEQ R1, R1, #1      ; executes ONLY if Z=1, else no-op
```

**Benefits:**
- **Saves 1 instruction word** (4 bytes in ARM mode).
- **Saves a pipeline flush** on taken branches.
- **Saves power** (no fetch/decode of the branch and the branch target's predecessor).
- Studies (ARM Whitepaper) show ~20–30% of all instructions in typical C code can use conditional suffixes, leading to ~10% overall code-size reduction and ~15% speedup.

**Barrel Shifter (3 marks):**
The barrel shifter allows shifts to be applied to **Operand-2** of any data-processing instruction **in the same cycle**, with zero latency. This converts what would be separate shift instructions on other ISAs into free operands.

**Example (1 mark):**
On x86, to compute `R0 = R1 + (R2 × 16)`, you would need:
```asm
SHL   R2, 4           ; 1 instruction
ADD   R0, R1, R2      ; 1 instruction   = 2 instructions
```

On ARM, the equivalent is:
```asm
ADD   R0, R1, R2, LSL #4   ; 1 instruction, 1 cycle!
```

This saves **one instruction** and one execution cycle. Across loops with thousands of iterations, this is a significant win.

**Combined Power-Density Effect (1 mark):**
Conditional execution + barrel shifting together mean that for the **same functionality**, ARM emits fewer, more efficient instructions → fewer memory fetches → lower dynamic power → longer battery life. This is the **single biggest reason** ARM dominates the mobile/embedded market.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Penalised Strictly by KTU Valuators):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing RISC with "fewer instructions":** RISC is about *regular, simple* instructions, not a *small count*. ARM has hundreds of instructions; the principle is uniformity and single-cycle execution, not minimalism.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to mention CPSR** when explaining conditional execution. The condition test operates on the **Current Program Status Register flags (N, Z, C, V)** — not on a separate flag register. Examiners **deduct 1 full mark** for omitting this.
> 
> 3. **Writing "Thumb is faster than ARM":** Wrong. **ARM mode is faster** (full instruction set, no decoding overhead). Thumb is **smaller and lower power**, not faster.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up R13/R14/R15:** R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC. Confusing these in a block diagram is an instant **1-mark penalty**.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** KTU strictly deducts **½ mark** for missing `MHz`, `ns`, `MIPS`, etc. in performance calculations.
> 
> 6. **In MIPS calculation:** Many students write `MIPS = N / (T × 10^6)`. While numerically correct in some forms, the **standard formula** $\text{MIPS} = f_{\text{clk}} / (\text{CPI} \times 10^6)$ is what KTU examiners expect. **Use the standard form.**
> 
> 7. **Omitting Amdahl's Law** when discussing speedup: If you compute a speedup of 2.4× in part of a program, you **must state the Amdahl limit** for the whole program. This is a frequent 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — ARM Design Philosophy**

- ✅ ARM = **Advanced RISC Machine** (originally Acorn RISC Machine).
- ✅ **RISC principles:** small, regular instruction set; hardwired control; single-cycle execution; load-store.
- ✅ **7 Pillars** to memorize: (1) Load-Store, (2) Fixed 32-bit width, (3) 16-register file, (4) Conditional exec, (5) Barrel shifter, (6) Simple addressing, (7) ARM/Thumb mode switch.
- ✅ **16 registers** because 4-bit fields fit the 32-bit encoding with room for shifter operands.
- ✅ **CPSR flags** (N, Z, C, V) drive conditional execution; condition field is bits **31:28**.
- ✅ **Thumb** = 16-bit, ~35% smaller, ~15% slower than ARM; switch via `BX` setting the **T-bit** in CPSR.
- ✅ **Thumb-2** mixes 16 and 32-bit encodings — used in **ARM Cortex-M** (microcontroller) cores.
- ✅ **Performance equation:** $T_{\text{CPU}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ — must be quoted in **every** performance question.
- ✅ **MIPS formula:** $\text{MIPS} = f_{\text{clk}} / (\text{CPI} \times 10^6)$.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/k]$ — apply when discussing speedup from any RISC feature.
- ✅ **Barrel shifter** sits in the data path; shifts are **0-cycle** (free).
- ✅ **Conditional execution** saves ~1 cycle per short branch by **gating the write-back stage** — not by skipping the fetch.
- ✅ **Load-store** means only `LDR`/`STR`/`LDM`/`STM` touch memory — never arithmetic instructions.
- ✅ **R13 = SP**, **R14 = LR**, **R15 = PC** — memorize for register-bank questions.
- ✅ **Key trade-off:** ARM (RISC) pays in **instruction count** but wins on **CPI, power, and clock frequency**.
- ✅ ARM dominates **mobile + embedded** because **performance-per-watt** beats CISC by an order of magnitude.
- ✅ For **KTU 14-mark answers**, always structure as: **(i) Concept → (ii) Why → (iii) Example → (iv) Trade-off.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded System Hardware - AMBA Bus Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Embedded System Hardware - AMBA Bus Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** is an open-standard, on-chip bus protocol originally developed by **ARM Holdings** in **1996**. It is a freely available specification that defines the communication infrastructure for connecting functional blocks (masters and slaves) inside a System-on-Chip (SoC). It standardizes the way processors, memory controllers, DMA engines, and peripherals exchange address, data, and control information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT631 Module 1):**
> AMBA is studied under *"Embedded System Hardware"*. Students must understand the **bus hierarchy**, **arbitration schemes**, **handshake protocols**, and the **differences between AHB, APB, ASB, and AXI**.

> [!NOTE]
> **ARM Definition:**
> "AMBA is a framework for designing high-performance embedded microcontrollers with multiple processors and peripherals, providing a standardized bus protocol for on-chip communication."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Highway System"

Imagine a **modern metropolitan city**:

- **CPU / DMA Controllers** = Delivery trucks originating from various warehouses (masters that *initiate* communication).
- **Memory & Peripherals** = Shops and storage facilities (slaves that *respond* to requests).
- **AMBA Bus** = The road network connecting them.
- **Arbiter** = A traffic police officer deciding which truck goes first at a junction.
- **Decoder** = A GPS unit that decodes the destination address and routes the truck to the right shop.
- **High-speed bus (AHB/AXI)** = A multi-lane expressway (for high-speed, heavy traffic).
- **Low-speed bus (APB)** = A narrow side lane (for small, infrequent deliveries like going to the bakery).

Just like a well-planned city needs a mix of expressways and service roads, an SoC needs a **hierarchical bus system** with both high-performance and low-power buses, interconnected via **bridges**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Standard Value / Description |
|------|------------------------------|
| AMBA Specification Year | **1996 (first version)**, latest AXI5/CHI-E released in 2023 |
| Bus Width | **32-bit, 64-bit, or 128-bit** (AHB supports up to 128-bit, AXI supports up to 1024-bit) |
| Clock Frequency | **Up to hundreds of MHz** in modern SoCs |
| Voltage Operation | **3.3 V (legacy)**, **1.8 V / 1.0 V (modern)** |
| Protocol Type | **Synchronous, on-chip** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AMBA became an industry standard:**
> It is **open-source, royalty-free**, supports a **modular hierarchical design**, and works seamlessly with ARM cores (Cortex-M, Cortex-A, Cortex-R) which form the backbone of billions of embedded devices.

---

## 1.4 The Need for AMBA — Background

In early microcontrollers (8051, PIC, basic ARM7), the CPU communicated with memory and peripherals using a **single shared bus** (Von Neumann architecture) or **simple peripheral busses**. As IC technology advanced:

- Multiple masters (CPU + DMA + DSP) needed simultaneous access.
- Peripherals had *vastly different bandwidth needs* (UART vs. DDR memory).
- Power consumption had to be minimized for mobile SoCs.
- Designers required **plug-and-play IP integration** from different vendors.

AMBA solved all these problems by introducing:
1. A **multi-master, multi-slave** architecture.
2. A **layered (hierarchical) bus system** — high-speed for memory/processors, low-speed for peripherals.
3. **Standardized handshaking** and bus arbitration protocols.
4. **IP reusability** — any third-party IP can integrate via AMBA without custom glue logic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of AMBA — Version Timeline

| AMBA Version | Year | Key Buses Introduced | Target Use |
|--------------|------|----------------------|------------|
| AMBA 1.0 | 1996 | **ASB, APB** | Basic embedded systems |
| AMBA 2.0 | 1999 | **AHB, ASB, APB** | High-performance, single-clock edge |
| AMBA 3.0 | 2003 | **AXI, AHB-Lite, APB** | High-frequency, pipelined, out-of-order |
| AMBA 4.0 | 2010 | **AXI4, AXI4-Lite, AXI4-Stream, ACE** | Coherent multi-core systems |
| AMBA 5.0 | 2013/2016 | **CHI, AXI5, AHB5** | Coherent hub interface, large SoCs |
| AMBA 6.0 | 2023 | **CHI-E, LTI** | Enhanced for AI/ML workloads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Most-Examined Buses: AHB, APB, AXI.** A student must know all three in depth.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of AMBA — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Advanced System Bus (ASB) — *The Origin*
- First generation high-performance bus.
- **Multi-master**, with built-in **bus arbiter** and **decoder**.
- Uses a **shared address bus** and **shared data bus** (with tri-state drivers).
- **Limitations:** Tri-state buses don't scale well at high frequencies → **superseded by AHB**.
- Still studied for *legacy* and *academic* purposes.

### 2.2.2 Advanced High-performance Bus (AHB)
- Introduced in AMBA 2.0 (1999).
- Designed for high-clock, high-bandwidth modules.
- **Key features:**
  * **Single-clock-edge protocol** — all transfers occur on the rising edge.
  * **Split transactions** — master can be released while slave prepares data.
  * **Burst transfers** — 4, 8, 16-beat bursts with incrementing/wrapping addressing.
  * **Pipelined address & data phases** (in newer versions).
  * **Up to 16 masters** supported via centralized arbitration.
  * **Bus widths:** 32, 64, 128 bits.

**AHB Signal Set (Master → Bus):**
`HADDR[31:0]`, `HTRANS[1:0]`, `HWRITE`, `HSIZE[2:0]`, `HBURST[2:0]`, `HPROT[3:0]`, `HWDATA[31:0]`, `HMASTLOCK`.

**AHB Signal Set (Slave → Bus):**
`HRDATA[31:0]`, `HREADY`, `HRESP[1:0]` (OKAY / ERROR / RETRY / SPLIT).

**AHB Signal Set (Arbiter):**
`HBUSREQx`, `HLOCKx`, `HGRANTx`, `HMASTER[3:0]`.

### 2.2.3 Advanced Peripheral Bus (APB)
- Designed for **low-bandwidth, low-power** peripherals (UART, GPIO, timers, I²C controllers).
- **No bus arbiter needed** — typically **one master (the AHB-to-APB bridge)**.
- **Unpipelined** — every transfer takes **at least 2 clock cycles** (setup phase + enable phase).
- **Very simple interface** — ideal for register-style peripherals.
- **Cost: minimal area and power.**

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the APB vs. AHB trade-off:**
> *"APB = **A**lways **P**atient and **B**asic."* Slow but simple and power-efficient.

### 2.2.4 Advanced eXtensible Interface (AXI)
- Introduced in AMBA 3.0 (2003) — the **most modern and widely used** AMBA bus.
- **Five independent channels** (instead of a shared bus):
  1. **AW** — Address Write channel
  2. **W** — Write Data channel
  3. **B** — Write Response channel
  4. **AR** — Address Read channel
  5. **R** — Read Data channel
- **Key features:**
  * **Separated address/response phases** → supports **out-of-order** completion.
  * **Outstanding transactions** — multiple addresses can be issued before data arrives.
  * **Burst mode with byte-select strobes** (WSTRB, RSTRB).
  * **Data interleaving** allowed.
  * **Quality of Service (QoS)** signals for priority.
  * **Optional user-defined signals.**
  * **AXI4-Lite:** lightweight version for register-mapped single-beat peripherals.
  * **AXI4-Stream:** for unidirectional streaming data (video, DSP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:**
> *"Compare AHB and AXI."* The biggest differences are:
> 1. AHB = shared bus; AXI = point-to-point channels.
> 2. AXI supports out-of-order and outstanding transactions.
> 3. AXI has separate read/write address channels.

## 2.3 Bus Architecture Topology

A typical AMBA-based SoC follows this **hierarchical structure**:

- **Top layer (AXI/AHB):** Connects the high-bandwidth blocks — CPU, DSP, GPU, external memory controller (DDR), DMA.
- **Bridge:** Translates AXI/AHB transactions to APB.
- **Bottom layer (APB):** Connects low-bandwidth peripherals — UART, SPI, I²C, GPIO, Timers, ADC, RTC, Watchdog.

> This is why a typical SoC block diagram looks like a "spider" with one central high-speed backbone and many low-speed branches.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Boundary Conditions

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---------------------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| Theoretical Bus Bandwidth | $BW = \dfrac{W \times f_{clk}}{N_{cycles}}$ | bits/second | $W$ = bus width, $f_{clk}$ = clock freq., $N_{cycles}$ = clocks per transfer |
| Transfer Time (Single Beat) | $T_{transfer} = N_{wait} \times T_{clk}$ | seconds | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ |
| Burst Transfer Time (AHB) | $T_{burst} = (N_{beats} + N_{wait}) \times T_{clk}$ | seconds | $N_{beats} = 4, 8, 16$ for AHB |
| APB Transfer Time | $T_{APB} = 2 \times T_{clk}$ (minimum) | seconds | Always $\geq$ 2 cycles |
| Bus Utilization | $U = \dfrac{T_{useful}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percent | $0\% \leq U \leq 100\%$ |
| Address Range | $A_{range} = 2^{N_{addr}}$ | bytes/locations | $N_{addr}$ = address lines |
| AMBA Address Space | $A_{space} = 2^{32}$ (4 GB) or $2^{64}$ | bytes | Most AMBA buses use 32-bit; AXI supports 64-bit |
| Arbitration Latency | $T_{arb} = N_{masters} \times T_{clk}$ | cycles | For round-robin arbiter (worst case) |
| Maximum Throughput (AXI) | $\Theta_{max} = W \times f_{clk} \times \text{(channel efficiency)}$ | bits/s | Channel efficiency $\leq 100\%$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> When calculating APB transfer time, **always include 2 cycles** even with zero wait states. APB's protocol *mandates* a setup phase.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs:** Qualcomm Snapdragon, MediaTek Dimensity, Apple A-series — all use AXI/CHI for internal fabric.
- **Microcontrollers:** STM32, NXP LPC, Infineon Aurix — use AHB + APB.
- **FPGA-based SoCs:** Xilinx Zynq, Intel Cyclone V — AXI4 is the de-facto fabric.
- **Automotive ADAS:** NXP S32K, TI Jacinto — use AMBA for safety-critical real-time processing.

> In short: **almost every ARM-based SoC you touch in industry uses AMBA as its communication backbone.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Analytical Walkthrough — AMBA Bus Read/Write Phases

### 3.1.1 AHB Burst Read Transfer (4-beat, incrementing)

A typical **4-beat incrementing burst read** on AHB proceeds in the following **timed states**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Phase 1: Address Phase (T1)} \\
&HCLK \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Master drives:} \\
&\quad \text{HADDR} = \text{Base Address (A)} \\
&\quad \text{HTRANS} = \text{NONSEQ} \quad (\text{first beat of burst, address not preceded by idle}) \\
&\quad \text{HBURST} = \text{INCR4} \quad (\text{4-beat incrementing burst}) \\
&\quad \text{HSIZE} = \text{WORD (32-bit)} \\
&\quad \text{HWRITE} = 0 \quad (\text{read operation})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Phase 2: Slave Decodes Address (T1 → T2)} \\
&\text{The address decoder activates the selected slave. The slave prepares data internally.} \\
&\text{Meanwhile, the arbiter may grant the bus to another master (split transaction).}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Phase 3: Data Phase 1 (T2)} \\
&HCLK \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Slave drives:} \\
&\quad \text{HRDATA} = \text{Memory}[A] \\
&\quad \text{HREADY} = 1 \quad (\text{data valid this cycle}) \\
&\text{Master samples HRDATA and latches Memory[A].}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Phase 4: Subsequent Burst Beats (T3, T4, T5)} \\
&\text{Master drives HTRANS = SEQ for beats 2, 3, 4.} \\
&\text{HADDR auto-increments: } A, A+4, A+8, A+12 \quad \text{(for 32-bit word bursts)} \\
&\text{Slave returns data for each address in the corresponding data phase.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Final Address (A + 12) Data Phase (T5)} \\
&\text{Slave drives HRDATA = Memory[A+12], HREADY = 1.} \\
&\text{Burst terminates; bus is released to arbiter.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Insertion of Wait States (HREADY = 0)

If the slave needs more time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{At } T_2: \text{HREADY} = 0 \Rightarrow \text{data is NOT yet valid. Master waits.} \\
&\text{At } T_3: \text{HREADY} = 1 \Rightarrow \text{data is valid and captured.} \\
&\text{Effective data phase = 2 cycles instead of 1.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total cycles for 4-beat burst with 1 wait state per beat:**

$$
T_{total} = (1 \text{ address phase}) + (4 \text{ data phases} \times 2 \text{ cycles}) = 9 \text{ cycles}
$$

Compare with **zero wait states:**

$$
T_{ideal} = 1 + 4 = 5 \text{ cycles}
$$

### 3.1.3 Bandwidth Calculation — Worked Example

**Problem:** A 32-bit AHB bus operates at 100 MHz. A 4-beat burst read is performed, with an average of 1 wait state per beat. Calculate the effective throughput.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bus width } W &= 32 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Clock frequency } f_{clk} &= 100 \text{ MHz} \\
\text{Clock period } T_{clk} &= \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Cycles per burst } &= 1 \text{ (address)} + 4 \times 2 \text{ (data with wait)} = 9 \text{ cycles} \\
\text{Bytes transferred per burst} &= 4 \text{ beats} \times 4 \text{ bytes/beat} = 16 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Burst duration} &= 9 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 90 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Throughput } \Theta &= \dfrac{16 \text{ bytes}}{90 \text{ ns}} = 0.1778 \text{ bytes/ns} = 177.8 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical peak} &= \dfrac{32 \text{ bits}}{10 \text{ ns}} = 3.2 \text{ Gbps} = 400 \text{ MB/s} \\
\text{Bus utilization } U &= \dfrac{177.8}{400} \times 100\% = 44.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 APB Transfer — Two-Phase Protocol

The APB protocol has a **rigid 2-phase structure** (or more, with wait states):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Phase 1: Setup Phase (T1)} \\
&\text{Bridge drives: } PADDR, PWRITE, PSEL, PENABLE = 0, PWDATA \\
&\text{Peripheral latches address/control. No data transfer yet.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Phase 2: Enable Phase (T2)} \\
&\text{Bridge asserts PENABLE = 1.} \\
&\text{If PWRITE = 1: peripheral latches PWDATA.} \\
&\text{If PWRITE = 0: peripheral drives PRDATA. PREADY = 1 (or 0 to wait).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**With zero wait states:**

$$
T_{APB} = 2 \times T_{clk}
$$

**With N wait states (slave drives PREADY = 0 for N extra cycles):**

$$
T_{APB} = (2 + N) \times T_{clk}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative AMBA Bus Performance

| Parameter | ASB | AHB | AXI4 | APB |
|-----------|-----|-----|------|-----|
| Max Frequency | ~100 MHz | ~200 MHz | ~1 GHz+ | ~50 MHz |
| Address Width | 32 bits | 32 bits | 64 bits (AXI4) | 32 bits |
| Data Width | 8–64 bits | 32, 64, 128 bits | 32–1024 bits | 8, 16, 32 bits |
| Bus Architecture | Shared | Shared | Point-to-point channels | Shared (single master) |
| Burst Support | No | Yes (4, 8, 16) | Yes (up to 256) | No |
| Pipelining | No | Yes (limited) | Full (5 channels) | No |
| Out-of-order | No | Limited (via SPLIT) | Yes (full) | No |
| Arbitration | Central | Central | Distributed (IDs) | None |
| Area Cost | Medium | Medium-High | High | Very Low |
| Power | Medium | High | Highest | Lowest |
| Use Case | Legacy | Embedded SoC | High-end SoC, FPGA | Peripheral Registers |

## 3.3 Arbitration Schemes in AMBA

AMBA defines a **centralized, priority-based arbiter** (for AHB) with these options:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{1. Fixed Priority} \\
&\text{Master M0 > M1 > M2 > ... > Mn} \\
&\text{Issue: Low-priority masters may starve (indefinitely wait).} \\
& \\
&\textbf{2. Round Robin} \\
&\text{Each master gets equal bus time. Starvation-free.} \\
&\text{Average wait time = } (N_{masters} - 1) \times T_{clk} / 2 \\
& \\
&\textbf{3. Time-Slice (Time-Division Multiplexed)} \\
&\text{Each master gets a fixed time window.} \\
& \\
&\textbf{4. Lottery / Random} \\
&\text{Used in some research designs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For AXI, the equivalent of arbitration is via **AWID/RID** (transaction IDs) and the **QoS signals** that allow slave-side prioritization. The fabric interconnect handles routing based on these.

## 3.4 Deriving the Maximum AMBA Bus Address Space

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of unique addresses} &= 2^{N_{addr}} \\
\text{If } N_{addr} &= 32 \\
\text{Address space} &= 2^{32} = 4,294,967,296 \text{ locations} \\
\text{If each location = 1 byte} &\Rightarrow 4 \text{ GB total} \\
\text{If } N_{addr} &= 64 \text{ (AXI4/5)} \\
\text{Address space} &= 2^{64} = 18.4 \text{ Exabytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

In a typical SoC, this 4 GB is partitioned:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROM} &: 0x00000000 \text{ to } 0x0FFFFFFF \quad (0 \text{–} 256 \text{ MB}) \\
\text{RAM} &: 0x20000000 \text{ to } 0x3FFFFFFF \quad (512 \text{ MB} \text{–} 1 \text{ GB}) \\
\text{Peripherals} &: 0x40000000 \text{ to } 0x5FFFFFFF \quad (512 \text{ MB}) \\
\text{External Memory} &: 0x60000000 \text{ to } 0xDFFFFFFF \\
\text{System Control} &: 0xE0000000 \text{ to } 0xFFFFFFFF
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Cortex-M memory map** which uses the AHB/APB fabric.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Mermaid Diagram — Full AMBA-Based SoC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU CORE ARM CORTEX"]
    DMA["DMA CONTROLLER"]
    DSP["DSP ENGINE"]
    AHB_M["AHB BUS MATRIX HIGH SPEED"]
    AHB2APB1["AHB TO APB BRIDGE 1"]
    AHB2APB2["AHB TO APB BRIDGE 2"]
    APB1["APB1 LOW SPEED BUS"]
    APB2["APB2 LOW SPEED BUS"]
    SRAM["INTERNAL SRAM 256KB"]
    FLASH["INTERNAL FLASH 1MB"]
    EXTMEM["EXTERNAL MEMORY CONTROLLER"]
    UART1["UART1 PERIPHERAL"]
    UART2["UART2 PERIPHERAL"]
    SPI1["SPI1 PERIPHERAL"]
    I2C1["I2C1 PERIPHERAL"]
    TIM1["TIMER1 PERIPHERAL"]
    ADC1["ADC1 PERIPHERAL"]
    GPIOA["GPIO PORT A"]
    GPIOB["GPIO PORT B"]
    ARB["CENTRAL ARBITER"]
    DEC["ADDRESS DECODER"]

    CPU --> AHB_M
    DMA --> AHB_M
    DSP --> AHB_M
    AHB_M --> ARB
    AHB_M --> DEC
    AHB_M --> SRAM
    AHB_M --> FLASH
    AHB_M --> EXTMEM
    AHB_M --> AHB2APB1
    AHB_M --> AHB2APB2
    AHB2APB1 --> APB1
    AHB2APB2 --> APB2
    APB1 --> UART1
    APB1 --> UART2
    APB1 --> SPI1
    APB1 --> I2C1
    APB2 --> TIM1
    APB2 --> ADC1
    APB2 --> GPIOA
    APB2 --> GPIOB
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — AMBA Bus Hierarchy (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L3["LAYER 3 APPLICATION CORES"]
    L2["LAYER 2 AXI OR AHB HIGH BANDWIDTH"]
    L1["LAYER 1 APB LOW POWER"]
    L0["LAYER 0 PHYSICAL PERIPHERALS"]
    BR["BRIDGE TRANSLATOR"]

    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> BR
    BR --> L1
    L1 --> L0
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — AXI 5-Channel Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["AXI MASTER"]
    S["AXI SLAVE"]

    M -->|AW ADDRESS WRITE| S
    M -->|W WRITE DATA| S
    S -->|B WRITE RESPONSE| M
    M -->|AR ADDRESS READ| S
    S -->|R READ DATA| M
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — AHB Arbitration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M0["MASTER 0 HIGH PRIORITY"]
    M1["MASTER 1 MEDIUM"]
    M2["MASTER 2 LOW"]
    ARB["BUS ARBITER"]
    BUS["AHB SHARED BUS"]
    SLV0["SLAVE 0 SRAM"]
    SLV1["SLAVE 1 FLASH"]
    SLV2["SLAVE 2 PERIPHERAL BRIDGE"]

    M0 --> ARB
    M1 --> ARB
    M2 --> ARB
    ARB --> BUS
    BUS --> SLV0
    BUS --> SLV1
    BUS --> SLV2
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — APB Transfer State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    IDLE --> SETUP : PSEL asserted
    SETUP --> ENABLE : PENABLE set on next rising edge
    ENABLE --> SETUP : more transfers
    ENABLE --> IDLE : transfer complete and no more
    ENABLE --> WAIT : PREADY equals 0
    WAIT --> ENABLE : PREADY equals 1
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram — AMBA Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["AMBA 1 1996 ASB APB"]
    A2["AMBA 2 1999 AHB ADDED"]
    A3["AMBA 3 2003 AXI INTRODUCED"]
    A4["AMBA 4 2010 ACE COHERENCY"]
    A5["AMBA 5 2016 CHI HUB"]
    A6["AMBA 6 2023 AXI5 CHI E"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid diagrams:** Each block represents a functional unit. Arrows represent **data flow / control signals**. The arbiter and decoder are special-purpose hardware modules, not software. In real SoC design, the AHB "bus matrix" replaces the older shared tri-state bus and acts as a *crossbar switch*.

---

## 4.7 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Hierarchical?

If we tried to connect **every** peripheral directly to the AHB:

- Bus load would be **too high** → can't drive so many inputs.
- Unused peripherals would consume **static power**.
- Critical peripherals (timers) would be **delayed** by non-critical DMA traffic.
- **Address decoding** logic would be massive.

The hierarchical AHB+APB structure with **bridges** solves all these:

- APB peripherals are gated (clock-gated) when not in use.
- Bridges buffer transactions and isolate the high-speed bus.
- Peripherals are grouped by clock domain (APB1 = low-speed, APB2 = high-speed peripherals).
- Address decoding is split between AHB decoder (coarse) and APB decoder (fine).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECPT631]`
**Define AMBA. List the major bus protocols defined under the AMBA standard.**

**Model Answer:**

> **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** is an open-standard on-chip interconnection protocol developed by ARM Ltd. in 1996, used to integrate processor, memory, and peripheral blocks within a System-on-Chip (SoC).

**Major AMBA Bus Protocols:**

1. **ASB (Advanced System Bus)** — first-generation, shared, used in legacy systems.
2. **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** — pipelined, burst-capable, used for high-bandwidth modules.
3. **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** — simple, low-power, for peripheral register access.
4. **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface)** — point-to-point, 5-channel, supports out-of-order transactions; dominant in modern SoCs.

> **Common Mistake:** Many students write "AMBA is a *chip*" or "AMBA is a *bus architecture by Intel*." AMBA is **on-chip bus specification by ARM**, **not** an IC itself.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECPT631]`
**Differentiate between AHB and APB in terms of bus architecture, transfer speed, and typical use.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus) | APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus) |
|-----------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Architecture | Multi-master, shared bus with arbiter | Single-master, shared bus |
| Transfer Speed | High (up to 200 MHz+) | Low (typically ≤ 50 MHz) |
| Burst Support | Yes (4, 8, 16 beats) | No |
| Pipelining | Yes (address + data phases) | No (2-cycle, non-pipelined) |
| Typical Use | CPU, RAM, DMA, external memory | UART, SPI, GPIO, Timers, ADC |

> **Mnemonic:** "AHB = **A**ccess **H**igh-speed **B**locks." "APB = **A**ccess **P**eripheral **B**locks."

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, PECPT631]`

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **AMBA AHB bus architecture** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **arbiter, decoder, and master-slave** in detail.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* With timing diagrams, explain the **AHB single-beat read transfer** and **4-beat incrementing burst read transfer**. Mention the significance of the `HREADY` and `HRESP` signals.

---

### Model Solution A(a) — AHB Architecture

**Step 1: Overview**

The AHB is a high-bandwidth bus in the AMBA family, designed for high-clock-frequency operation. It connects high-performance modules such as the **CPU, on-chip SRAM, external memory controller, and DMA**.

**Step 2: Key Components (Block Diagram)**

```
+----------------+      +----------------+      +----------------+
|  Master 1 CPU  |      |  Master 2 DMA  |      |  Master 3 DSP  |
+--------+-------+      +--------+-------+      +--------+-------+
         \                       |                       /
          \                      |                      /
           +---------------------+---------------------+
                                 |
                       +---------v---------+
                       |    ARBITER        |
                       |  (bus request,    |
                       |   grant, lock)    |
                       +---------+---------+
                                 |
                                 | Grant signals
                                 v
                       +---------+---------+
                       |   ADDRESS BUS     |
                       |   + DATA BUS      |
                       |   + CONTROL       |
                       +---------+---------+
                                 |
                       +---------v---------+
                       |   DECODER         |
                       |  (HSELx logic)    |
                       +---------+---------+
                                 |
       +-------------------------+-------------------------+
       |                         |                         |
+------v------+           +------v------+           +------v------+
|  Slave 1    |           |  Slave 2    |           |  Slave 3    |
|  SRAM       |           |  External   |           |  AHB→APB    |
|             |           |  Memory     |           |  Bridge     |
+-------------+           +-------------+           +-------------+
```

**Step 3: Role of Arbiter**

The **arbiter** is a centralized hardware block that:
- Receives bus requests (`HBUSREQx`) from all masters.
- Uses a **priority scheme** (fixed, round-robin, or mixed).
- Asserts `HGRANTx` to one master at a time.
- Supports **bus locking** (`HLOCKx`) for atomic operations.
- Performs **bus parking** — granting the bus to a default master during idle.

**Step 4: Role of Address Decoder**

The decoder:
- Monitors the **HADDR bus** continuously.
- Asserts the appropriate `HSELx` line to enable the selected slave.
- The address map is hard-coded or register-configurable.

**Step 5: Role of Masters and Slaves**

| Element | Function |
|---------|----------|
| **Master** | Initiates read/write; provides address & control. Examples: CPU, DMA, DSP, GPU. |
| **Slave** | Responds to address; provides data. Examples: Memory, AHB-to-APB bridge. |
| **Arbiter** | Decides which master gets the bus. |
| **Decoder** | Routes the address to the correct slave. |

---

### Model Solution A(b) — AHB Transfers

**Step 1: Single-Beat Read Transfer**

A single read takes 2 phases (no wait states):

| T# | HTRANS | HRESP | HREADY | HRDATA |
|----|--------|-------|--------|--------|
| T1 | NONSEQ | — | 1 | — |
| T2 | — | OKAY | 1 | Data |

- **T1 (Address phase):** Master drives HADDR, HTRANS=NONSEQ, HWRITE=0, HSIZE=WORD.
- **T2 (Data phase):** Slave drives HRDATA with the read data. HREADY=1 indicates valid data.

**Step 2: 4-Beat Incrementing Burst Read**

| T# | HTRANS | HADDR | HRDATA | HREADY |
|----|--------|-------|--------|--------|
| T1 | NONSEQ | A | — | 1 |
| T2 | SEQ | A+4 | Mem[A] | 1 |
| T3 | SEQ | A+8 | Mem[A+4] | 1 |
| T4 | SEQ | A+12 | Mem[A+8] | 1 |
| T5 | IDLE | — | Mem[A+12] | 1 |

**Step 3: Significance of HREADY**

- `HREADY = 1` → data is **valid this cycle**, master latches it.
- `HREADY = 0` → slave needs **more time**; the entire bus is held (address and data both extended).

**Step 4: Significance of HRESP**

- `HRESP = OKAY (00)` → transfer successful.
- `HRESP = ERROR (01)` → slave reports an error.
- `HRESP = RETRY (10)` → slave asks master to retry the **same transaction** later.
- `HRESP = SPLIT (11)` → slave asks master to retry, but allows the master to do other things in the meantime (split-transaction protocol).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECPT631]`

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Describe the **AMBA AXI protocol** in detail. Explain its **5 independent channels** and how it differs from AHB.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* With a neat diagram, explain the **hierarchical bus structure** in a typical AMBA-based SoC. How does the AHB-to-APB bridge function?

---

### Model Solution B(a) — AXI Protocol

**Step 1: What is AXI?**

**AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface)** is the third-generation AMBA bus (AMBA 3.0, 2003). It is a **point-to-point, channel-based protocol** designed for high-frequency, high-bandwidth operation in modern SoCs. It supports:
- Separate read/write address channels.
- Out-of-order transaction completion.
- Burst transfers with byte strobes.
- Quality of Service (QoS).
- Optional user-defined signals.

**Step 2: The 5 Independent Channels**

| Channel | Direction | Purpose | Key Signals |
|---------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| **AW** (Address Write) | Master → Slave | Write address | `AWADDR`, `AWVALID`, `AWREADY`, `AWSIZE`, `AWBURST`, `AWPROT`, `AWID`, `AWLEN`, `AWQOS` |
| **W** (Write Data) | Master → Slave | Write data | `WDATA`, `WVALID`, `WREADY`, `WSTRB`, `WLAST` |
| **B** (Write Response) | Slave → Master | Completion of write | `BRESP`, `BVALID`, `BREADY`, `BID` |
| **AR** (Address Read) | Master → Slave | Read address | `ARADDR`, `ARVALID`, `ARREADY`, `ARSIZE`, `ARBURST`, `ARID`, `ARLEN`, `ARQOS` |
| **R** (Read Data) | Slave → Master | Read data | `RDATA`, `RVALID`, `RREADY`, `RLAST`, `RRESP`, `RID` |

**Step 3: Handshake Mechanism (VALID + READY)**

A channel transfer occurs when **both `VALID` and `READY` are HIGH on the same rising clock edge**.

$$
T_{transfer} = T_{VALID\_asserted} + T_{READY\_asserted} - T_{overlap}
$$

If `VALID` is asserted first, the slave must accept when `READY` is asserted → source-first handshake.
If `READY` is asserted first, master can assert `VALID` anytime → destination-first handshake.

**Step 4: AXI vs. AHB — Key Differences**

| Feature | AHB | AXI |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Topology | Shared bus | Point-to-point channels |
| Address/Data Phase | Single shared bus | Separate channels |
| Outstanding Trans. | No (1 active at a time per master) | Yes (multiple simultaneously) |
| Out-of-Order Completion | Limited (via SPLIT) | Yes (via ID matching) |
| Burst Length | 4, 8, 16 | 1–256 (AXI4) |
| Byte Strobe | No (HSIZE only) | Yes (WSTRB) |
| QoS / Priority | No | Yes (4-bit) |
| Interleaving | No | Allowed |

---

### Model Solution B(b) — Hierarchical Bus Structure

**Step 1: Why Hierarchy?**

Different SoC blocks have different bandwidth, power, and timing requirements. A single bus cannot efficiently serve all of them. AMBA solves this by using a **two-tier (or three-tier) hierarchy**:

**Step 2: Block Diagram**

```
   +---------------------------------------------+
   |             HIGH-SPEED FABRIC (AXI/AHB)      |
   |                                              |
   |  +-------+   +-------+   +-------+          |
   |  | CPU   |   | DMA   |   | DSP   |          |
   |  +---+---+   +---+---+   +---+---+          |
   |      |           |           |              |
   |      +-----------+-----------+              |
   |                  |                          |
   |     +------------v-------------+            |
   |     |   AHB BUS MATRIX         |            |
   |     +---+--------+--------+----+            |
   |         |        |        |                 |
   |     +---v---+ +--v---+ +--v---+             |
   |     | SRAM  | | FLASH| | DDR  |             |
   |     +-------+ +------+ +------+             |
   |                                              |
   +------------------+--------------------------+
                      |
             +--------v----------+
             |  AHB → APB BRIDGE |
             +---------+---------+
                       |
        +--------------+---------------+
        |              |               |
   +----v----+    +----v----+    +-----v-----+
   | APB1    |    | APB2    |    | APB3      |
   | UART    |    | SPI     |    | GPIO      |
   | I2C     |    | TIMERS  |    | ADC       |
   +---------+    +---------+    +-----------+
```

**Step 3: How the AHB-to-APB Bridge Works**

The bridge is a **protocol translator** with the following behavior:

1. **Accepts an AHB transaction** from the high-speed bus (e.g., a CPU write to a peripheral register).
2. **Translates the address** to the APB address space (often by address-mask comparison).
3. **Decomposes the single AHB transfer** into **2 APB cycles**: SETUP and ENABLE.
4. **Generates APB control signals**: `PSELx`, `PENABLE`, `PWRITE`, `PADDR`, `PWDATA`.
5. **Holds AHB** (`HREADY = 0`) until the APB transfer completes (`PREADY = 1`).
6. **Returns HREADY = 1** to release the AHB bus.

**Step 4: Advantages of the Bridge**

- **Power saving:** APB peripherals can be clock-gated.
- **Protocol isolation:** AHB timing constraints don't propagate to APB.
- **Frequency scaling:** APB can run at a lower frequency (e.g., HCLK/2 or HCLK/4).
- **Ease of peripheral design:** APB slaves are simple to design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Do not confuse AXI4 with AXI4-Lite.** AXI4-Lite is *single-beat only* (no burst), used for register access. AXI4 supports bursts up to 256 beats.
> 2. **Do not write "AMBA is made by Intel" or "AMBA is for external memory."** It is by **ARM**, and is strictly **on-chip**.
> 3. **In the AHB timing diagram, never label the address and data as "occurring at the same edge."** AHB is **synchronous on the rising edge**, with address phase **one cycle before** the data phase.
> 4. **For APB, do not say "1 cycle per transfer."** The minimum is **2 cycles** (SETUP + ENABLE). A common KTU trick question asks: *"What is the APB transfer time with zero wait states at 50 MHz?"* → Answer: 40 ns, not 20 ns.
> 5. **Always mention the role of the arbiter and decoder** explicitly. Marks are awarded for naming them.
> 6. **Draw clean block diagrams** with arrow direction (master → slave, response → master). A diagram without proper arrows loses easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Core Concept Bullets
- ✅ **AMBA = Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture** by **ARM** (1996).
- ✅ AMBA is an **open, on-chip bus standard** — royalty-free.
- ✅ It defines a **hierarchical** bus structure: high-speed backbone + low-speed peripheral bus.
- ✅ **ASB** = first-generation high-performance bus; **legacy**, tri-state shared.
- ✅ **AHB** = current industry-standard high-performance bus; **burst support**, **split transactions**, single-edge clocked.
- ✅ **APB** = low-power peripheral bus; **no bursts**, **2-cycle minimum transfer**, no arbiter needed.
- ✅ **AXI** = modern, 5-channel, point-to-point, supports **out-of-order** and **outstanding** transactions; dominant in modern SoCs.
- ✅ **AXI4** supports bursts up to 256 beats; **AXI4-Lite** is single-beat only; **AXI4-Stream** is unidirectional data.
- ✅ A typical SoC has **AXI/AHB** for CPU, memory, DMA; **APB** for peripherals.
- ✅ **AHB-to-APB bridge** = protocol translator + clock domain crossing.

### Critical Signals to Memorize
- ✅ AHB: `HADDR`, `HTRANS`, `HBURST`, `HSIZE`, `HWRITE`, `HWDATA`, `HRDATA`, `HREADY`, `HRESP`, `HBUSREQ`, `HGRANT`, `HSELx`.
- ✅ APB: `PADDR`, `PWRITE`, `PSEL`, `PENABLE`, `PWDATA`, `PRDATA`, `PREADY`.
- ✅ AXI: `AWADDR/WDATA/ARADDR/RDATA`, `*VALID`, `*READY`, `*BURST`, `*SIZE`, `*LEN`, `WSTRB`, `BRESP/RRESP`.

### Key Numerical Facts
- ✅ AMBA address space = **2³² = 4 GB** (for 32-bit AHB/APB), **2⁶⁴** (for AXI4/5).
- ✅ AHB burst = **4, 8, or 16** beats.
- ✅ APB transfer = **≥ 2 cycles** (always).
- ✅ AXI4 burst = **up to 256** beats.
- ✅ AXI4-Lite = **1 beat** only (no bursts).
- ✅ APB minimum transfer at 50 MHz = **40 ns** (NOT 20 ns).

### Must-Know Comparisons
- ✅ **AHB vs APB** — speed, arbitration, burst, pipelining.
- ✅ **AHB vs AXI** — channels, out-of-order, interleaving, ID.
- ✅ **AMBA 1 vs 2 vs 3 vs 4 vs 5** — feature additions in each generation.

### Common Real-World Examples (Write in Exams for Extra Marks)
- ✅ **STM32F4 series** uses **AHB + APB** (multi-APB buses: APB1 = 42 MHz, APB2 = 84 MHz).
- ✅ **NXP i.MX8** uses **AXI4** as main SoC fabric.
- ✅ **Xilinx Zynq-7000** uses **AXI4** for PS-PL communication.
- ✅ **Qualcomm Snapdragon** uses **CHI** for coherent multi-core.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When the question says *"Explain AMBA,"* always structure your answer as: **Definition → Need → Buses (ASB/AHB/APB/AXI) → Architecture diagram → Typical transfer (timing) → Comparison table → Real-world example.** This is the **valuation-key pattern** that KTU examiners reward with full marks.

---
*End of Module 1 Notes — Embedded System Hardware: AMBA Bus Protocol*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded System Software Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Embedded System Software Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Embedded System Software** refers to the specialized programming instructions, firmware, operating logic, and middleware that execute on a microcontroller or microprocessor embedded within a larger electromechanical or electronic device to perform a dedicated, real-time, and resource-constrained function. Unlike general-purpose computing software, embedded software is tightly coupled with the hardware, is typically stored in non-volatile memory (ROM/Flash), and is designed to operate under strict constraints of power, memory, timing, and reliability.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECPT631 - Advanced Microcontrollers)** framework, embedded system software applications are categorized based on their functional role within a microcontroller-based system — ranging from low-level device drivers and bootloader code to high-level application logic, real-time operating system (RTOS) kernels, and middleware protocols.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Embedded System Software Applications cover the taxonomy of software that runs on advanced microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M, PIC, AVR, MSP430 families), the design considerations for firmware, and the classification of software by application domain (automotive, industrial, consumer, medical, IoT).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of an **embedded system as a specialized restaurant kitchen**:
- The **microcontroller** is the *head chef* — it executes only the recipes (instructions) given to it.
- The **embedded software** is the *recipe book* — pre-written, deterministic, and designed for one type of cuisine.
- The **hardware peripherals** (sensors, actuators, timers) are the *cooking tools* — driven by the chef.
- The **RTOS** (if present) is the *kitchen manager* — coordinating multiple chefs (tasks) to ensure each dish (job) is served on time.

Just as a restaurant kitchen is not a general-purpose computer but is optimized to serve specific dishes quickly and repeatedly, embedded system software is not designed for arbitrary user tasks but for **one specific function, executed reliably, often in real-time**.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Embedded Software

| Characteristic | Description | KTU Exam Relevance |
|----------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Determinism** | Same input produces the same output within a known time bound | Frequently asked (3-mark/7-mark) |
| **Resource Constraint** | Operates with limited RAM (e.g., 4–512 KB), Flash (e.g., 16 KB–2 MB) | Important in design questions |
| **Real-Time Response** | Must respond to events within a deadline (hard or soft real-time) | Central to RTOS questions |
| **Reliability** | Often cannot be rebooted easily; must run for years without failure | Critical for safety systems |
| **Power Efficiency** | Designed for low-power modes (sleep, deep sleep, idle) | High-yield topic |
| **Hardware Dependence** | Tightly bound to specific MCU architecture (ARM, RISC-V, etc.) | Foundation concept |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Hard Real-Time Deadline:** Failure to meet = system failure (e.g., airbag deployment, anti-lock braking).
> - **Soft Real-Time Deadline:** Failure degrades quality but not safety (e.g., video streaming buffer).
> - **Memory Footprint:** Typical embedded firmware = **4 KB – 256 KB**.
> - **Power Budget:** IoT-class devices aim for **< 1 mW average power**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Advanced Microcontrollers

The **PECPT631** course focuses on advanced microcontrollers such as **ARM Cortex-M3/M4, STM32, ESP32, and SAMD families**. The software that runs on these microcontrollers is what gives them their "intelligence." Understanding software applications enables students to:

1. Design **firmware architectures** for complex embedded products.
2. Select appropriate **RTOS or bare-metal** approaches.
3. Implement **communication stacks** (I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, USB).
4. Develop **IoT, motor control, and sensor fusion** applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Software Architecture Layers in an Embedded System

Embedded system software is structured in **hierarchical layers**, each abstracting the layer below it. This modularization aids portability, maintainability, and reusability.

### Layer 1: Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)
- The lowest software layer.
- Provides **register-level access** to MCU peripherals (GPIO, ADC, Timers, UART).
- Example: STM32 HAL functions like `HAL_GPIO_WritePin()`.
- **Why?** Allows upper layers to remain unchanged when porting to a different MCU.

### Layer 2: Device Drivers
- Software modules that control specific peripheral devices (sensors, LCD, EEPROM, motors).
- Interact with hardware via HAL or direct register manipulation.
- Example: Driver for an MPU6050 gyroscope over I2C.

### Layer 3: Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Kernel *(Optional but common)*
- Provides **task scheduling, inter-task communication, and synchronization**.
- Examples: **FreeRTOS, RTX, Zephyr, Contiki**.
- Services: Semaphores, Mutexes, Queues, Timers, Event Groups.

### Layer 4: Middleware
- Protocol stacks (TCP/IP, MQTT, CANopen, Modbus), file systems (FATFS), and graphics libraries (LVGL).
- Sits between RTOS and application code.

### Layer 5: Application Layer
- The product-specific logic — the "brain" of the system.
- Example: PID control algorithm for a drone, state machine for a washing machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When asked "Explain the software architecture of an embedded system," always present these 5 layers with a one-line description of each.

## 2.2 Classification of Embedded Software Applications

Embedded software applications are classified by **functional domain** and **timing criticality**.

### By Functional Domain:

| Domain | Example Application | Typical MCU | Key Software Feature |
|--------|--------------------|-----------|--------------------|
| **Automotive** | ECU, ABS, Airbag, Infotainment | ARM Cortex-M4, Aurix TC2xx | AUTOSAR, OSEK/VDX RTOS, CAN stack |
| **Industrial** | PLC, Motor Drives, SCADA | STM32F4, TI C2000 | Modbus, EtherCAT, PID control |
| **Consumer** | Smart TV Remote, Washing Machine, Toys | ESP32, PIC16, AVR | Low-power states, capacitive touch |
| **Medical** | Pacemaker, Glucometer, Infotusion Pump | MSP430, ARM Cortex-M0 | IEC 62304 compliance, watchdog |
| **IoT / Wireless** | Smart Sensor, Wearable, Home Automation | ESP32, nRF52, CC2530 | MQTT, CoAP, BLE, LoRa stacks |
| **Aerospace & Defense** | Flight Controller, Missile Guidance | ARM Cortex-R, PowerPC | DO-178C, redundant execution |

### By Timing Criticality:

1. **Hard Real-Time Embedded Software** — deterministic deadlines, zero tolerance for jitter.
2. **Soft Real-Time Embedded Software** — deadlines are desirable but not catastrophic.
3. **Best-Effort (Non-Real-Time)** — e.g., firmware update, user interface rendering.

## 2.3 Bare-Metal vs. RTOS-Based Firmware

A central design decision in embedded software is whether to run **bare-metal** (super-loop / event-driven) or use an **RTOS**.

### Bare-Metal Super-Loop Model

$$T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{task,i} + T_{idle}$$

Where:
- $T_{cycle}$ = total loop execution time
- $T_{task,i}$ = execution time of task $i$
- $T_{idle}$ = idle time before loop restart
- $n$ = number of tasks

**Limitation:** Longest-case response time is unbounded if any task becomes slow.

### RTOS Preemptive Scheduling Model

$$R_{max} = T_{WCET} + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_{max}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot T_{WCET,j}$$

This is the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** equation for fixed-priority preemptive scheduling, where:
- $R_{max}$ = worst-case response time of task $i$
- $T_{WCET}$ = Worst-Case Execution Time
- $hp(i)$ = set of higher-priority tasks
- $T_j$ = period of higher-priority task $j$

A task is **schedulable** if and only if $R_{max} \leq D_i$ (its deadline).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Use Case |
|---------|---------------|-------|----------|
| **CPU Utilization (RMS)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | dimensionless | Check if $n$ tasks fit on 1 CPU |
| **Deadline Monotonic Bound** | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ for $n$ tasks | dimensionless | Schedulability test |
| **Power Consumption** | $P = V \cdot I \cdot f_{active} + P_{sleep}$ | mW | Battery-life estimation |
| **Battery Life** | $L_{hours} = \frac{C_{mAh} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{avg}}$ | hours | IoT device planning |
| **Interrupt Latency** | $L = N_{cycles} \cdot T_{clk}$ | $\mu s$ | Real-time response |
| **Baud Rate Error** | $E\% = \left\lvert \frac{f_{actual} - f_{desired}}{f_{desired}} \right\vert \times 100$ | % | UART reliability |
| **Timer Period** | $T_{timer} = \frac{PSC \cdot ARR}{f_{clk}}$ | seconds | STM32 timer config |
| **ADC Sampling Rate** | $f_s = \frac{f_{ADC\_clk}}{N_{cycles} + 12.5}$ | samples/s | Sensor acquisition |
| **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D = \frac{CCR}{ARR + 1} \times 100\%$ | % | Motor/servo control |
| **Firmware Size Constraint** | $S_{firmware} \leq S_{Flash}$ | bytes | Memory budgeting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When answering numericals, always state the **assumptions** clearly (e.g., "Assume idle power is negligible" or "Assume 8-MHz clock with no prescaler"). Unstated assumptions = lost marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Embedded system software is the **backbone of modern industry**:
- A modern car contains **>100 ECUs** running **>100 million lines of code**.
- A Boeing 787 Dreamliner has **~6.5 million lines** of embedded code.
- Smart factories depend on **embedded PLCs** running deterministic control loops.
- The **IoT market (USD 1.6 trillion in 2025)** is entirely driven by embedded software on resource-constrained microcontrollers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Utilization Bound

The **Liu & Layland Theorem (1973)** provides the upper bound on CPU utilization for a set of $n$ periodic tasks scheduled under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), where task priority is inversely proportional to its period.

**Given:**
- $n$ periodic tasks, each with:
  - $T_i$ = period (time between successive releases of task $i$)
  - $C_i$ = worst-case execution time of task $i$

**To Derive:** The maximum total CPU utilization $U_{max}$ for which all tasks meet their deadlines.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define utilization for a single task.**

$$U_i = \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

**Step 2 — Define total utilization for $n$ tasks.**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

**Step 3 — State the schedulability sufficient condition.**

A sufficient (but not necessary) condition for all tasks to meet their deadlines under RMS is:

$$U \leq U_{bound}(n)$$

**Step 4 — Derive the bound using worst-case phasing.**

The worst-case scenario occurs when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with the task under analysis. Through combinatorial analysis (detailed in Liu & Layland, 1973), the bound converges to:

$$U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

**Step 5 — Verify the limit as $n \to \infty$.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

Using the identity $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^x - 1}{x} = \ln 2$, and substituting $x = \frac{1}{n}$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

**Step 6 — Tabulate common values of $U_{bound}(n)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{bound}(1) &= 1 \cdot (2^{1} - 1) = 1.0000 \\
U_{bound}(2) &= 2 \cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1) = 0.8284 \\
U_{bound}(3) &= 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 0.7798 \\
U_{bound}(4) &= 4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) = 0.7568 \\
U_{bound}(5) &= 5 \cdot (2^{1/5} - 1) = 0.7435 \\
U_{bound}(\infty) &= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even with infinitely many tasks, RMS can theoretically schedule them as long as total CPU utilization stays below **~69.3%**. In practice, designers aim for **< 70% utilization** to leave headroom for interrupts, context switches, and system services.

## 3.2 Worked Example: RMS Schedulability Test

**Problem:** An embedded system has 3 periodic tasks with the following parameters. Check if they are schedulable under RMS.

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Priority (by RMS) |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| $\tau_1$ | 10 | 2 | Highest (smallest T) |
| $\tau_2$ | 20 | 5 | Medium |
| $\tau_3$ | 50 | 8 | Lowest |

**Step 1 — Compute utilization of each task.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 \\
U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 \\
U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{8}{50} = 0.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total utilization.**

$$U_{total} = 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.16 = 0.61$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the bound for $n = 3$.**

$$U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 0.7798$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

$$U_{total} = 0.61 \leq U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SCHEDULABLE}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with Response Time Analysis (RTA).**

For each task, iteratively compute $R_i^{k+1} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^k}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ starting with $R_i^0 = C_i$.

For $\tau_3$ (lowest priority, $hp = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^0 &= 8 \\
R_3^1 &= 8 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 8 + 1(2) + 1(5) = 15 \\
R_3^2 &= 8 + \left\lceil \frac{15}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{15}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 8 + 2(2) + 1(5) = 17 \\
R_3^3 &= 8 + \left\lceil \frac{17}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{17}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 8 + 2(2) + 1(5) = 17
\end{aligned}
$$

Fixed point reached: $R_3 = 17$ ms $\leq D_3 = T_3 = 50$ ms. ✓ **Schedulable.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bare-Metal vs. RTOS Trade-offs

| Criterion | Bare-Metal (Super-Loop) | RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS) |
|-----------|------------------------|-----------------------|
| **Code Size** | 1 – 10 KB | 4 – 30 KB |
| **RAM Usage** | Minimal (no stacks per task) | High (separate stack per task) |
| **Determinism** | High for simple cases | Depends on scheduler |
| **Scalability** | Poor (task additions break timing) | Excellent (modular tasks) |
| **Power Management** | Manual (developer-controlled) | Built-in idle hooks |
| **Development Time** | Fast for tiny projects | Slower initial setup, faster scaling |
| **Debugging Complexity** | Low | Medium-High (priority inversion, deadlock) |
| **KTU Recommended Use** | LED blink, simple sensor read | Multi-sensor IoT node, motor control + comms |

## 3.4 Power Optimization Equation for Battery-Powered IoT

For a duty-cycled IoT node:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{T_{active} \cdot P_{active} + T_{sleep} \cdot P_{sleep}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$$

**Where:**
- $T_{active}$ = duration of active transmission/processing per cycle
- $P_{active}$ = power during active mode (e.g., ESP32 transmitting ≈ 240 mW)
- $T_{sleep}$ = deep sleep duration
- $P_{sleep}$ = sleep power (ESP32 deep sleep ≈ 10 $\mu$W)

**Example:** ESP32 wakes every 60 s, transmits for 3 s at 240 mW, sleeps at 10 $\mu$W.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \frac{3 \cdot 240 \cdot 10^{-3} + 57 \cdot 10 \cdot 10^{-6}}{60} \\
&= \frac{0.720 + 0.000570}{60} \\
&= \frac{0.72057}{60} \approx 0.01201 \text{ W} = 12.01 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Battery life on a 2000 mAh, 3.7 V Li-Po cell (energy = 7.4 Wh):

$$L_{hours} = \frac{7.4}{0.01201} \approx 616 \text{ hours} \approx 25.7 \text{ days}$$

This kind of analysis is a **favorite KTU numerical question** under "embedded software applications for IoT."

## 4.1 Layered Software Architecture of an Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["APPLICATION LAYER"] --> A2["Product Specific Logic: PID Control, State Machine, Data Processing"]
    B1["MIDDLEWARE LAYER"] --> B2["Protocol Stacks: MQTT, CAN, Modbus, FATFS, LVGL"]
    C1["RTOS KERNEL LAYER"] --> C2["Task Scheduler, Semaphores, Queues, Timers, ISR Management"]
    D1["DEVICE DRIVER LAYER"] --> D2["Peripheral Drivers: GPIO, ADC, UART, I2C, SPI, PWM, DMA"]
    E1["HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER"] --> E2["Register Level Access: Memory Mapped IO, CMSIS, HAL Functions"]
    F1["HARDWARE LAYER"] --> F2["MCU Core, Memory, Peripherals, Sensors, Actuators"]

    A2 --> B2
    B2 --> C2
    C2 --> D2
    D2 --> E2
    E2 --> F2
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BM["BARE METAL SUPER LOOP"]

        R3 --> R6["Task C: Wireless Transmit"]
        R4 --> R3
        R5 --> R3
        R6 --> R3
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between hard real-time and soft real-time embedded systems with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Deadline Nature** | Absolute; missing it is a system failure | Statistical; missing degrades quality |
| **Consequence of Miss** | Catastrophic (loss of life, money) | Tolerable (minor performance drop) |
| **Example 1** | Airbag deployment ECU in a car | Video streaming on a smart TV |
| **Example 2** | Pacemaker heart stimulator | Smartphone touch screen response |
| **Design Priority** | Determinism over throughput | Throughput over absolute timing |

**Example explanation:** A pacemaker must deliver an electrical pulse within a few milliseconds of detecting an abnormal heartbeat — failure is fatal. In contrast, a video frame dropping occasionally is acceptable; the user perceives a slight glitch but the system remains functional.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: What is an RTOS? List any four features of FreeRTOS used in embedded microcontrollers.**

**Model Answer:**

**RTOS (Real-Time Operating System)** is a lightweight operating system designed to manage hardware resources and execute multiple tasks on a microcontroller with strict timing guarantees.

**Four features of FreeRTOS:**
1. **Preemptive and Cooperative Scheduling** — supports both priority-based preemptive and round-robin cooperative modes.
2. **Task Management** — allows creation of multiple tasks, each with its own stack and priority.
3. **Inter-Task Communication** — provides queues, semaphores, mutexes, and event groups for synchronization.
4. **Software Timers** — enables creation of timers that call callbacks without consuming hardware timer resources.
5. **Tick Hooks and Idle Hooks** — let developers add custom code during system tick and idle time for power management.

*(Any four points suffice for full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice 1*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Paper PECPT631)]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the layered software architecture of an embedded system with a neat diagram. Describe the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how the Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) algorithm is used to check schedulability of periodic real-time tasks. A system has 3 tasks with periods and execution times as: Task 1: T = 20 ms, C = 4 ms; Task 2: T = 30 ms, C = 6 ms; Task 3: T = 50 ms, C = 8 ms. Determine if the task set is schedulable under RMS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The embedded system software is organized in **five layers**, from closest to hardware (lowest abstraction) to farthest (highest abstraction):

**1. Hardware Layer:**
The physical microcontroller (MCU), memory chips (Flash, SRAM), and external peripherals (sensors, actuators). Example: STM32F407VG with 1 MB Flash, 192 KB SRAM.

**2. Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):**
Provides register-level functions to read/write MCU peripherals in a portable way. Example: STM32 HAL functions like `HAL_UART_Transmit()`, `HAL_ADC_Start()`. Decouples application code from hardware specifics.

**3. Device Driver Layer:**
Software modules that initialize and control specific external devices. Examples: I2C driver for MPU6050 IMU, SPI driver for SD card, GPIO driver for LED matrix.

**4. RTOS Kernel Layer:** *(optional)*
Provides multitasking services — task creation, scheduling, semaphores, queues. Examples: FreeRTOS, Zephyr. Used in complex applications with multiple concurrent activities.

**5. Middleware Layer:**
Protocol stacks and utility libraries used by the application. Examples: TCP/IP stack (lwIP), MQTT client, FATFS file system, LVGL graphics.

**6. Application Layer:**
The product-specific logic. Examples: PID control loop for a drone, menu navigation for a washing machine, alarm-decision algorithm for a smoke detector.

*(Diagram to be drawn as shown in Section 4.1 — Layered Software Architecture.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is a fixed-priority preemptive algorithm where **shorter period = higher priority**. A sufficient schedulability test (Liu & Layland, 1973) is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

**Given Data:**

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Priority (RMS) |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| $\tau_1$ | 20 | 4 | Highest |
| $\tau_2$ | 30 | 6 | Medium |
| $\tau_3$ | 50 | 8 | Lowest |

**Step 1 — Compute individual utilizations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{4}{20} = 0.20 \\
U_2 &= \frac{6}{30} = 0.20 \\
U_3 &= \frac{8}{50} = 0.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization.**

$$U_{total} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.16 = 0.56$$

**Step 3 — Bound for $n = 3$ tasks.**

$$U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

$$U_{total} = 0.56 \leq 0.7798 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SCHEDULABLE under RMS}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using RTA for lowest priority task $\tau_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^0 &= 8 \\
R_3^1 &= 8 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{30} \right\rceil \cdot 6 = 8 + 1(4) + 1(6) = 18 \\
R_3^2 &= 8 + \left\lceil \frac{18}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{18}{30} \right\rceil \cdot 6 = 8 + 1(4) + 1(6) = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

Fixed point reached: $R_3 = 18$ ms $\leq D_3 = 50$ ms. ✓ **Schedulable confirmed.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice 2*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper PECPT631)]**

**Q: (a)** Compare bare-metal firmware design with RTOS-based firmware design in embedded systems. Discuss at least six points and state one application scenario where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT sensor node based on ESP32 operates in duty-cycled mode. It wakes every 120 seconds, performs sensing and WiFi transmission for 4 seconds at an active power of 280 mW, then enters deep sleep drawing 8 $\mu$W. The node is powered by a 2500 mAh, 3.7 V Li-Po battery. Estimate the average power consumption and battery lifetime in days. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Comparison Point | Bare-Metal Firmware | RTOS-Based Firmware |
|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **1. Code Structure** | Single super-loop `while(1)` with sequential task calls | Multiple concurrent tasks managed by scheduler |
| **2. Memory Footprint** | Very small (1–10 KB Flash, < 1 KB RAM) | Larger (4–30 KB Flash, several KB RAM for task stacks) |
| **3. Timing Determinism** | Depends on worst-case loop time; can be predictable for small systems | Scheduler adds overhead but provides bounded response time |
| **4. Scalability** | Poor — adding tasks risks breaking timing guarantees | Excellent — new tasks added with defined priorities |
| **5. Development Complexity** | Low — easy for beginners, fast prototyping | High — requires understanding of priorities, deadlocks |
| **6. Power Management** | Manual — developer writes sleep code explicitly | Built-in — idle hook runs when no task is ready |
| **7. Inter-Task Sync** | Flags, global variables (race conditions) | Semaphores, mutexes, queues (safe primitives) |
| **8. Preferred Use Case** | Simple applications: LED blink, temperature logger with single sensor | Complex systems: drone flight controller, automotive ECU, smart home gateway |

**Preferred Scenarios:**
- **Bare-metal preferred:** A standalone digital thermometer that reads a sensor every 5 seconds and displays on an LCD — no concurrency needed, ultra-low-power, minimal cost.
- **RTOS preferred:** A wearable fitness tracker that simultaneously (i) samples heart rate, (ii) counts steps via accelerometer, (iii) updates a BLE link to a phone, and (iv) handles button presses — concurrent tasks with real-time deadlines.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Wake cycle: $T_{cycle} = 120$ s
- Active duration: $T_{active} = 4$ s
- Sleep duration: $T_{sleep} = 120 - 4 = 116$ s
- Active power: $P_{active} = 280$ mW
- Sleep power: $P_{sleep} = 8 \, \mu W = 0.008$ mW
- Battery: $C = 2500$ mAh, $V = 3.7$ V
- Battery energy: $E = V \cdot C = 3.7 \cdot 2500 = 9250$ mWh = 9.25 Wh

**Step 1 — Compute average power using duty cycle formula.**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{T_{active} \cdot P_{active} + T_{sleep} \cdot P_{sleep}}{T_{cycle}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \frac{4 \cdot 280 + 116 \cdot 0.008}{120} \\
&= \frac{1120 + 0.928}{120} \\
&= \frac{1120.928}{120} \\
&\approx 9.341 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to current draw.**

$$I_{avg} = \frac{P_{avg}}{V} = \frac{9.341 \text{ mW}}{3.7 \text{ V}} \approx 2.524 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Compute battery life in hours.**

$$L_{hours} = \frac{C_{mAh}}{I_{mA}} = \frac{2500}{2.524} \approx 990.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to days.**

$$L_{days} = \frac{990.5}{24} \approx 41.27 \text{ days}$$

**Final Answer:** Average power ≈ **9.34 mW**, Battery lifetime ≈ **41.3 days**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students often write sleep power in mW while active is in mW — both must be in the **same unit** (convert $\mu W$ to mW: $8 \, \mu W = 0.008$ mW).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mWh to Wh** when the answer requires days; keep all units consistent throughout.
> 3. **Mixing up $T_{active}$ and $T_{cycle}$** in the duty cycle formula — always use $T_{cycle}$ as the denominator.
> 4. **Skipping Step 1 justification** — write the formula *before* substituting, even if it seems obvious. Examiners reward methodology.
> 5. **No final boxed answer** — KTU expects the numerical result to be clearly stated (e.g., **"Battery lifetime ≈ 41.3 days"**).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Embedded System Software Applications**

- **Definition:** Embedded software = specialized code running on an MCU to perform a dedicated, often real-time function.
- **Key Characteristics:** Determinism, resource constraint, real-time response, reliability, low power, hardware dependence.
- **Five-Layer Architecture:** Application → Middleware → RTOS Kernel → Device Drivers → HAL → Hardware.
- **Bare-Metal vs RTOS:** Bare-metal = small footprint, low overhead, no concurrency. RTOS = scalable, concurrent, real-time, higher overhead.
- **Hard vs Soft Real-Time:** Hard = zero tolerance for deadline miss (pacemaker, ABS). Soft = graceful degradation (video playback).
- **RMS Priority Rule:** Shorter period → higher priority (preemptive, fixed-priority).
- **Liu & Layland Bound:** $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; limit = $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$.
