- **Application Domains:** Automotive, Industrial, Consumer, Medical, IoT, Aerospace — each with distinct software standards (AUTOSAR, IEC 62304, DO-178C).
- **Power Formula:** $P_{avg} = \frac{T_{active} \cdot P_{active} + T_{sleep} \cdot P_{sleep}}{T_{cycle}}$ — frequently tested.
- **Battery Life Formula:** $L_{hours} = \frac{C_{mAh} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{avg}}$.
- **Common MCUs:** ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4, STM32, ESP32, MSP430, PIC, AVR, nRF52.
- **Common RTOS:** FreeRTOS, Zephyr, RTX, Contiki, NuttX.
- **Toolchains:** Keil MDK, STM32CubeIDE, IAR Embedded Workbench, PlatformIO, GCC ARM.
- **Exam Mantra:** Always (1) state the formula, (2) substitute values with units, (3) show intermediate steps, (4) box the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Core Data Flow Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** ARM Core Data Flow Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧠 ARM Core Data Flow Model — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **ARM Core Data Flow Model** is a block-diagrammatic representation of the internal architecture of an ARM processor (typically the ARM7TDMI reference design) that illustrates how instructions, operands, and addresses traverse the processor datapath during a single instruction cycle. It explicitly maps the journey of data between the **Register Bank**, the **Barrel Shifter**, the **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)**, the **Address Register / Incrementer**, and the **external memory buses**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The data flow model is the *conceptual backbone* for understanding pipelining, instruction execution, addressing modes, and exception handling in ARM. Every subsequent topic (LPC2148, GPIO, Timer, UART, Interrupts) implicitly depends on understanding this datapath.

The model exposes four distinct data movement operations performed during an instruction cycle:

1. **Operand Fetch (Read Register)** – Operands are read from the Register Bank.
2. **Operand Shift (Barrel Shifter)** – The second operand is shifted/rotated before ALU entry.
3. **ALU Operation** – Arithmetic or logical operation is performed.
4. **Result Write-back** – The ALU output is routed either to the destination register OR to the Address Register for memory access.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line"

Imagine the ARM core as a **highly optimized car assembly line inside a single factory room**:

| Factory Element | ARM Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Parts warehouse rack | Register Bank (R0–R15) | Stores raw operands |
| Cutting/reshaping machine | Barrel Shifter | Modifies the second operand (shift/rotate) |
| Robotic welder | ALU | Combines the two operands |
| Painted output conveyor | Result Write-back bus | Sends ALU result to destination |
| Delivery truck dispatch | Address Register & Increment | Sends result to memory & advances PC |

The **single-clock cycle** is analogous to *one complete assembly cycle* — a single car rolls off the line every cycle. This is what makes ARM an elegant **RISC** architecture: one cycle, one predictable datapath traversal.

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standards

- **Word size:** **32 bits** for the ARM datapath (registers, ALU, buses).
- **Number of general-purpose registers visible to programmer:** **31** (R0–R12 user-mode + R13 SP, R14 LR, R15 PC).
- **Register Bank read ports:** **2** (operands A and B).
- **Register Bank write port:** **1** (write-back).
- **Barrel shifter operations per cycle:** **1** (shift amount 0–31, or rotate amount 0–31).
- **Pipeline stages (ARM7TDMI):** **3** (Fetch, Decode, Execute).

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"data flow model"** is a *teaching abstraction*. Real silicon implements additional features (caches, MMU/MPU, write buffers), but the KTU syllabus deliberately uses this simplified model to expose the essential mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Datapath Components

The ARM Core Data Flow Model comprises the following major functional blocks connected by multiplexers and bidirectional buses.

## 2.1 The Register Bank

The Register Bank is a **dual-ported read, single-ported write** memory holding **31 × 32-bit** general-purpose registers plus the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** and **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** for exception modes.

- **Operand A bus** → feeds the ALU directly.
- **Operand B bus** → feeds the Barrel Shifter input.
- Any of R0–R15 can be used as a base register for addressing (especially R15 = PC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 2 read ports?** Most ARM data-processing instructions are **3-address** (e.g., `ADD R0, R1, R2`). This requires fetching **two source operands** simultaneously in one cycle — hence the dual read ports.

## 2.2 The Barrel Shifter

The Barrel Shifter is a combinational logic block that performs one of **five** operations on the Operand B value *before* it enters the ALU, **in a single clock cycle**:

| Operation | Mnemonic | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Logical Shift Left | `LSL` | Shift left, fill LSBs with 0 |
| Logical Shift Right | `LSR` | Shift right, fill MSBs with 0 |
| Arithmetic Shift Right | `ASR` | Shift right, **replicate sign bit** |
| Rotate Right | `ROR` | Rotate bits right, LSB → MSB |
| Rotate Right Extended | `RRX` | Rotate right through the **C flag** |

The shift amount is specified by an immediate (0–31) in the instruction OR by the low-order byte of a register (Rs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The Barrel Shifter is *not* a separate instruction stage. It resides **inside the Execute stage** of the pipeline. This is why every ARM data-processing instruction *implicitly* supports a shift on its second operand *for free*.

## 2.3 The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)

The ALU receives two 32-bit inputs:
- **Operand A** → directly from Register Bank.
- **Operand B** → from the Barrel Shifter output.

It produces:
- A 32-bit **result**.
- **Flags** (N, Z, C, V) which may update the CPSR if the instruction suffix `S` is set.

## 2.4 The Address Register & Incrementer

This block handles **PC-relative addressing** and **register-offset addressing** for Load/Store instructions. It computes the effective memory address (base + offset) and contains dedicated increment logic that adds **+4** to the PC during every Fetch stage, supporting the sequential pipeline.

## 2.5 The Write-Back / Result Multiplexer

After the ALU produces its result, a multiplexer routes the result to one of two destinations:
- **Destination register** in the Register Bank (data-processing result).
- **Address Register** (for load/store address calculation).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit / Boundary | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effective Address (register offset) | $EA = R_n + \text{shift}(R_m)$ | $EA$ in bytes | Load/Store addressing |
| PC increment per instruction | $PC_{new} = PC_{old} + 4$ | Bytes (ARM = 32-bit aligned) | Sequential pipelining |
| Logical Shift Left (×N) | $X_{\text{LSL}\,N} = X \cdot 2^{N}$ | $N \in [0, 31]$ | Multiply by powers of 2 in 1 cycle |
| Logical Shift Right (÷N) | $X_{\text{LSR}\,N} = \lfloor X / 2^{N} \rfloor$ | $N \in [1, 32]$ | Divide by powers of 2 |
| Barrel Shifter input selection | $B_{shifted} = f(B, \text{shift\_type}, \text{shift\_amount})$ | 32-bit result | Single-cycle shifts |
| ALU result (ADD) | $R_d = A + B_{shifted}$ | 32-bit, with carry-out | Arithmetic ops |
| ALU result (SUB) | $R_d = A - B_{shifted}$ | 32-bit, with borrow | Comparison/subtract |
| Carry flag (ADD) | $C = \text{cout}(A + B_{shifted})$ | Boolean | Multi-precision arithmetic |
| Overflow flag (ADD) | $V = (A_{31} \oplus R_{d\,31}) \cdot (B_{31} \oplus R_{d\,31})$ | Boolean | Signed arithmetic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This datapath enables single-cycle Multiply-by-2 instructions (`LSL #1`), single-cycle divide-by-2, single-cycle mask generation, and efficient array indexing — making ARM extremely efficient for DSP, control loops, and bit-manipulation tasks.

## 2.7 Why the Data Flow Model Matters in Engineering

- **Compiler writers** use this model to optimize register allocation.
- **RTOS designers** rely on it for context-switch latency prediction.
- **Embedded firmware engineers** (LPC2148, STM32) use the shift semantics to write efficient GPIO, timer, and interrupt handlers.
- **Digital VLSI designers** extend this model to add caches, branch predictors, and write buffers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: PC Increment Operation

The PC must advance by one instruction word (= 4 bytes, since ARM is fixed 32-bit) per cycle. However, during a **branch** instruction, the PC is loaded with the branch target address. Let us derive the general expression for PC value at any cycle *n*.

**Step 1 — Initial State (Reset).**

$$PC_0 = \text{Reset\_Vector\_Address} = 0\text{x}00000000$$

**Step 2 — Sequential Increment Rule.**

On every Fetch cycle (assuming no branch taken), the incrementer adds 4:

$$PC_{n+1} = PC_n + 4$$

**Step 3 — General Term (Sequential Flow).**

By induction, after *k* sequential cycles:

$$PC_k = PC_0 + 4k$$

**Step 4 — Branch Override.**

If a branch is taken at cycle *n*, the incrementer output is **discarded** and the branch target $T$ is loaded:

$$PC_{n+1} = T$$

**Step 5 — Combined Decision Equation.**

The general PC update rule can be written as a piecewise expression:

$$
PC_{n+1} =
\begin{cases}
PC_n + 4 & \text{if no branch at cycle } n \\
T & \text{if branch taken at cycle } n
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This piecewise behavior is exactly what the ARM pipeline's "Decode stage" decides, and is the foundation of why ARM branch instructions incur a **3-cycle penalty** in the classic ARM7TDMI pipeline (Fetch, Decode, Execute empty during branch).

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Address Calculation (Register Offset)

For a Load/Store instruction such as `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]`:

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**
- Base register = $R_1$
- Offset register = $R_2$
- Shift = Logical Shift Left by 2

**Step 2 — Apply the Barrel Shifter operation on the offset register.**

$$R_2^{\text{shifted}} = R_2 \ll 2 = R_2 \cdot 2^{2} = 4 \cdot R_2$$

**Step 3 — Feed to Address Register (which acts as an adder for address calculation).**

$$EA = R_1 + R_2^{\text{shifted}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2.**

$$EA = R_1 + 4 \cdot R_2$$

**Step 5 — Final general form.**

$$
EA = R_n + \text{BarrelShifter}(R_m, \text{type}, \text{amount})
$$

**Numerical Example.** Suppose $R_1 = 0\text{x}20000000$ and $R_2 = 0\text{x}00000010$ (= 16 decimal).

$$
EA = 0\text{x}20000000 + (0\text{x}10 \ll 2)
$$

$$
EA = 0\text{x}20000000 + 0\text{x}40
$$

$$
EA = 0\text{x}20000040
$$

This is the address that will be placed on the **A-bus** during the memory-access phase.

## 3.3 Derivation: Logical Shift Left as Multiplication

For `LSL #N`:

**Step 1 — Binary representation of X.**

$$X = \sum_{i=0}^{31} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$$

**Step 2 — Shift left by N (fill LSBs with 0).**

$$X \ll N = \sum_{i=0}^{31-N} b_i \cdot 2^{i+N}$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $2^N$.**

$$X \ll N = 2^{N} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{31-N} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the original $X$ pattern (top bits discarded).**

For $N \le 31$, the result equals:

$$X_{\text{LSL}\,N} = X \cdot 2^{N} \pmod{2^{32}}$$

Hence `LSL #N` performs **multiply by $2^N$** in a single cycle.

**Worked numerical example.** Let $X = 0\text{x}00000005$ (= 5), $N = 3$.

$$
X_{\text{LSL}\,3} = 5 \cdot 2^{3} = 40
$$

In binary: $0000\,0101 \ll 3 = 0010\,1000 = 0\text{x}28 = 40$. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Datapath Component Summary

| Datapath Block | Input(s) | Output(s) | Width | Cycle Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Register Bank (Read Port 1) | Register address (Rn) | Operand A | 32-bit | Combinational read |
| Register Bank (Read Port 2) | Register address (Rm) | Operand B | 32-bit | Combinational read |
| Barrel Shifter | Operand B + shift control | Shifted Operand B | 32-bit | Combinational shift |
| ALU | Operand A + Shifted Operand B | Result + Flags | 32-bit + 4 flags | Combinational |
| Address Register & Incrementer | Base + Offset / PC | Effective Address | 32-bit | Latched / Incremented |
| Write-Back MUX | ALU result or Memory data | Destination data | 32-bit | Routed to Rd |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams — Datapath Architecture

## 4.1 ARM Core Data Flow Model — Top-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MEM[MEMORY SYSTEM]
        addrBus[A-Bus Address Lines]
        dataBus[D-Bus Data Lines]
        instrMem[(Instruction Memory)]
        dataMem[(Data Memory)]
    end

    subgraph REG[REGISTER BANK]
        r0[R0]
        r1[R1]
        r2[R2]
        r3[R3]
        r4[R4]
        r5[R5]
        r6[R6]
        r7[R7]
        r8[R8]
        r9[R9]
        r10[R10]
        r11[R11]
        r12[R12]
        r13[R13 SP]
        r14[R14 LR]
        r15[R15 PC]
    end

    subgraph SHIFT[BARREL SHIFTER]
        shifterUnit[LSL LSR ASR ROR RRX Engine]
    end

    subgraph ALU_BLOCK[ALU CORE]
        aluEngine[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
        flagReg[CPSR Flags]
    end

    subgraph ADDR[ADDRESS UNIT]
        addrReg[Address Register]
        incLogic[PC Increment Plus 4]
    end

    r0 --> r15

    instrMem --> r15
    r15 --> incLogic
    incLogic --> r15

    r0 --> r1 --> r2 --> r3 --> r4 --> r5 --> r6 --> r7 --> r8 --> r9 --> r10 --> r11 --> r12 --> r13 --> r14

    r0 -->|Operand A| aluEngine
    r1 -->|Operand A| aluEngine
    r2 -->|Operand A| aluEngine
    r3 -->|Operand A| aluEngine
    r4 -->|Operand A| aluEngine
    r5 -->|Operand A| aluEngine

    r0 -->|Operand B| shifterUnit
    r1 -->|Operand B| shifterUnit
    r2 -->|Operand B| shifterUnit
    r3 -->|Operand B| shifterUnit
    r4 -->|Operand B| shifterUnit

    shifterUnit -->|Shifted Operand B| aluEngine
    aluEngine --> flagReg
    aluEngine -->|Result| r0
    aluEngine -->|Result| r1
    aluEngine -->|Result| r2
    aluEngine -->|Result| r3
    aluEngine -->|Result| r4

    aluEngine -->|Result| addrReg
    addrReg --> addrBus
    addrBus --> dataMem
    dataMem --> dataBus
    dataBus --> r0
```

## 4.2 Instruction Execution Cycle — Stage-by-Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stageA[FETCH STAGE] --> stageB[DECODE STAGE]
    stageB --> stageC[EXECUTE STAGE]

    stageA --> fetchOps1[Read Instruction from Memory using PC]
    stageA --> fetchOps2[PC = PC plus 4 in Address Incrementer]

    stageB --> decodeOps1[Decode Instruction Opcode]
    stageB --> decodeOps2[Read Source Registers from Register Bank]
    stageB --> decodeOps3[Generate Barrel Shifter Control Signals]

    stageC --> execOps1[Barrel Shifter Produces Shifted Operand B]
    stageC --> execOps2[ALU Performs Operation]
    stageC --> execOps3[Flag Update if S bit Set]
    stageC --> execOps4[Result Routed via Multiplexer]
    stageC --> execOps5[Write Back to Destination Register OR Address Register]
```

## 4.3 Datapath — Functional Block-Level Architecture (Detailed View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INSTRUCTION_FETCH[FETCH SUBSYSTEM]
        pcNode[R15 PC Register]
        incNode[PC Incrementer Adds 4]
        memA[Instruction Memory Interface]
        pcNode --> incNode
        incNode --> pcNode
        pcNode --> memA
    end

    subgraph DECODE[DECODE SUBSYSTEM]
        decNode[Instruction Decoder]
        regRd[Register Bank Read Ports]
        ctrlNode[Control Signal Generator]
        memA --> decNode
        decNode --> regRd
        decNode --> ctrlNode
    end

    subgraph EXECUTE[EXECUTE SUBSYSTEM]
        shiftNode[Barrel Shifter]
        aluNode[ALU]
        addrNode[Address Register]
        flagNode[Flag Update Logic]
        wbMux[Write Back Multiplexer]

        regRd -->|Operand A| aluNode
        regRd -->|Operand B| shiftNode
        shiftNode -->|Shifted B| aluNode
        ctrlNode --> shiftNode
        ctrlNode --> aluNode
        aluNode --> flagNode
        aluNode --> wbMux
        wbMux --> addrNode
        wbMux --> regRd
    end
```

## 4.4 Multiplexer Routing Logic for Write-Back

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    wbSelect{Write Back Destination Select}

    wbSelect -->|Bit Q equals 0| regWrite[Route ALU Result to Destination Register Rd]
    wbSelect -->|Bit Q equals 1| addrOut[Route Result to Address Register for Memory Access]

    regWrite --> regBack[Register Bank Write Port]
    addrOut --> memBus[External Address Bus]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**Explain the role of the Barrel Shifter in the ARM Core Data Flow Model. Why is it placed inside the Execute stage rather than as a separate pipeline stage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Barrel Shifter is a combinational block that performs a **shift or rotate** on the second ALU operand *before* the ALU operation, **in a single clock cycle**. Its operations include LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, and RRX with shift amounts 0–31.

It is placed inside the **Execute stage** rather than as a separate stage because:
1. It operates **combinationally** — its output settles within one ALU delay.
2. Adding it as a separate stage would **double** the pipeline depth, increasing branch penalty and data hazard cost.
3. Most instructions (data-processing) require only one operand to be shifted, so a dedicated stage would be **idle for half the cycles**.

**Mark allocation:** Definition (1) + Operations list (1) + Justification (1).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**List the major functional blocks of the ARM Core Data Flow Model. Why does the Register Bank have two read ports and one write port?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Major functional blocks:
1. Register Bank (R0–R15 + CPSR)
2. Barrel Shifter
3. ALU
4. Address Register & Incrementer
5. Write-back Multiplexer
6. Memory Buses (Address bus, Data bus)

The Register Bank has **two read ports** because most ARM data-processing instructions are **3-address format** (`ADD Rd, Rn, Rm`) requiring two source operands to be fetched simultaneously. The **single write port** is sufficient because the result is always written to **one destination** register (Rd) per instruction, completing the RISC philosophy.

**Mark allocation:** List (1) + 2-port explanation (1) + 1-port explanation (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**(a)** Draw and explain the ARM Core Data Flow Model with all major functional blocks. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With the help of an example instruction, explain how the Barrel Shifter processes the second operand before ALU execution. Show how `LSL` implements multiplication by powers of 2. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Diagram and Explanation — 7 marks**

Draw the block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 / 4.3 mermaid diagram converted to hand-drawing style in exam). Clearly label:

- **Register Bank** with R0–R15, two read ports A & B, one write port. (1 mark)
- **Barrel Shifter** receiving Operand B with shift control. (1 mark)
- **ALU** receiving Operand A directly and Operand B via shifter. (1 mark)
- **Flag logic** updating CPSR based on ALU outputs. (1 mark)
- **Address Register and PC Incrementer** handling effective addresses. (1 mark)
- **Write-back multiplexer** routing result to Rd or Address Register. (1 mark)
- **External memory buses** (A-bus for address, D-bus for data). (1 mark)

**Explanation points (cover in 3-4 lines):**
- During Fetch, PC is read and incremented by 4.
- During Decode, source registers are read into the two read ports.
- During Execute, the shifter modifies Operand B, the ALU computes, and the result is written back or routed to the Address Register.

**(b) Barrel Shifter Example — 7 marks**

Example: `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #2`

This means: $R_0 = R_1 + (R_2 \ll 2)$

**Step-by-step:**
1. Operand A = $R_1$ read from port 1.
2. Operand B = $R_2$ read from port 2.
3. **Barrel Shifter** executes `LSL #2` on $R_2$ combinationally:
   - Each bit of $R_2$ is shifted left by 2 positions.
   - LSBs (bits 0, 1) are filled with 0.
   - MSBs (bits 31, 30) are discarded (lost).

4. The shifted value equals $R_2 \cdot 4$.
5. ALU performs ADD: $R_0 = R_1 + 4 \cdot R_2$.

**Numerical example.** $R_1 = 0\text{x}20000000$, $R_2 = 0\text{x}00000010$.

$$
R_2 \ll 2 = 0\text{x}00000040
$$

$$
R_0 = 0\text{x}20000000 + 0\text{x}00000040 = 0\text{x}20000040
$$

**LSL implements multiplication by powers of 2:** A left shift of N positions is mathematically equivalent to multiplying by $2^N$ (modulo $2^{32}$). Therefore `LSL #3` multiplies by 8, `LSL #4` by 16, and so on. This gives ARM a **free single-cycle multiply-by-power-of-2** without invoking the hardware multiplier.

**Mark allocation:** Decode example (2) + Shifter trace (2) + ALU computation (1) + LSL = multiplication derivation (2).

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1] *(Alternative Choice)*
**(a)** Explain the function of the Program Counter (R15) in the ARM Core Data Flow Model. Derive the general expression for PC update during sequential and branch flows. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the effective address calculation for the Load/Store instruction `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #3]` given $R_1 = 0\text{x}40000000$ and $R_2 = 0\text{x}00000100$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Program Counter Function — 7 marks**

The **PC (R15)** serves dual roles:
1. **Address source** for instruction fetch.
2. **General-purpose register** readable/writable by instructions (one pipeline-stage offset is normally observed).

**Sequential PC update derivation:**

$$
PC_{n+1} = PC_n + 4
$$

For *k* sequential cycles:

$$
PC_k = PC_0 + 4k
$$

**Branch PC update:**

$$
PC_{n+1} = T \quad \text{(branch target address)}
$$

**Combined decision expression:**

$$
PC_{n+1} =
\begin{cases}
PC_n + 4 & \text{if branch not taken} \\
T & \text{if branch taken}
\end{cases}
$$

The PC incrementer inside the Address Register subsystem computes $PC_n + 4$ in the same cycle that the instruction is being fetched, ensuring continuous pipelined execution.

**Mark allocation:** Dual role (1) + Sequential derivation (2) + Branch derivation (2) + Combined expression (2).

**(b) Effective Address Calculation — 7 marks**

Instruction: `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #3]`

Given: $R_1 = 0\text{x}40000000$, $R_2 = 0\text{x}00000100$.

**Step 1:** Identify operands.
- Base register = $R_1$ (value = $0\text{x}40000000$)
- Offset register = $R_2$ (value = $0\text{x}00000100$)
- Shift = LSL #3 (multiply by 8)

**Step 2:** Apply Barrel Shifter on $R_2$.

$$
R_2 \ll 3 = 0\text{x}00000100 \cdot 8 = 0\text{x}00000800
$$

Verification in binary:

$$
0000\,0001\,0000\,0000 \ll 3 = 0000\,1000\,0000\,0000 = 0\text{x}0800
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Effective Address.

$$
EA = R_1 + R_2^{\text{shifted}}
$$

$$
EA = 0\text{x}40000000 + 0\text{x}00000800
$$

$$
EA = 0\text{x}40000800
$$

**Step 4:** Memory access.
- Address $0\text{x}40000800$ is placed on the **A-bus**.
- The 32-bit word at that location is fetched via the **D-bus**.
- The word is routed through the write-back multiplexer to **R0**.

**Step 5:** Pipeline interaction.
- During Execute stage of LDR, the Address Register holds $0\text{x}40000800$.
- During the memory-access phase (extended Execute cycle for LDR), the data returns from memory and is written back to R0 in the next cycle.

**Mark allocation:** Operand identification (1) + Barrel shifter step (2) + EA computation (2) + Memory access explanation (1) + Pipeline note (1).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the shifter with a separate stage.** Many students draw an extra "Barrel Shifter Stage" in the pipeline diagram. The shifter is **inside the Execute stage** — combinationally cascaded with the ALU.
> 2. **Writing `LSL` as "shift right."** `LSL` = Logical Shift **Left** (multiply). `LSR` = Logical Shift **Right** (divide). Confusing these loses 2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Forgetting PC + 4 vs PC + 8.** ARM increments PC by **4 bytes** (one 32-bit word), NOT by 8. Thumb mode uses +2, but in ARM state it is **always +4**.
> 4. **Ignoring the dual read port requirement.** If you describe a Register Bank with only one read port, you will lose 2 marks because the data flow model fundamentally relies on simultaneous dual operand fetch.
> 5. **Skipping the shifter in LDR addressing.** Effective address calculations such as `[R1, R2, LSL #N]` *must* pass through the Barrel Shifter. Direct ALU addition without showing the shifter step loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Not labelling buses.** The Address bus (A-bus) and Data bus (D-bus) must be distinct in your diagram. Many students merge them, losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ The **ARM Core Data Flow Model** is a teaching abstraction showing how one ARM instruction moves through the core in **one clock cycle**.
- ✅ Five major blocks: **Register Bank**, **Barrel Shifter**, **ALU**, **Address Register & Incrementer**, **Write-Back MUX**.
- ✅ **Register Bank** = 31 user-visible registers (R0–R15) + CPSR + SPSRs; **dual-read, single-write**.
- ✅ **Barrel Shifter** = 5 operations (LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX), combinational, **0–31** shift amount, **inside Execute stage**.
- ✅ **LSL #N = multiply by $2^N$** in 1 cycle; **LSR #N = unsigned divide by $2^N$**.
- ✅ **ALU** = combinational, 32-bit, 4 flags (N, Z, C, V) — flags updated only when `S` bit set.
- ✅ **PC (R15)** increments by **+4 bytes** in sequential flow; replaced by branch target on taken branch.
- ✅ **Effective Address** for `[Rn, Rm, shift]` = $R_n + \text{BarrelShifter}(R_m)$.
- ✅ Three-stage pipeline: **Fetch → Decode → Execute**, each stage = 1 cycle.
- ✅ Write-back multiplexer routes ALU result either to **destination register** OR to **Address Register** for memory access.
- ✅ The entire datapath is **32 bits wide** — word-aligned, fixed instruction size.
- ✅ Understanding this model is **prerequisite** for all subsequent modules: GPIO, Timers, UART, Interrupts, ADC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Registers # 📘 Registers in Advanced Microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M Architecture) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 🎯 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Register (Computer Architecture Definition):** > A **register** is a high-speed, small-capacity storage element composed of a bank of **D flip-flops** (or latches) integrated directly inside the **CPU core**. Each register has a fixed bit-width (typically **32 bits** in ARM Cortex-M) and is accessed in a single clock cycle. Registers are the *fastest* memory tier in the computer hierarchy, sitting at **Tier-0**, immediately accessible to the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) without any bus arbitration or memory-mapped address cycles. In the context of **PECPT631 – Advanced Microcontrollers**, the term *Registers* specifically refers to the **ARM Cortex-M3 / Cortex-M4 programmer's model register set**, which consists of: 1. **General-Purpose Registers (GPRs):** R0 – R12 (13 registers, all 32-bit) 2. **Special-Purpose Core Registers:** R13 (SP), R14 (LR), R15 (PC) 3. **Program Status Registers (PSRs):** APSR, IPSR, EPSR 4. **Special Mask & Control Registers:** PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI, CONTROL --- ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workbench" Intuition Imagine a **carpenter's workbench**: | Workbench Analogy | Microcontroller Equivalent | |-------------------|----------------------------| | 🪚 **Tool rack on the wall** (slow to fetch) | External **Flash / RAM** memory | | 🧰 **Toolbox on the bench** (moderate speed) | **Memory-mapped peripheral registers** | | ✋ **Tools already in the carpenter's hands** (instant access) | **CPU Core Registers (R0–R15)** | A master carpenter never puts down the hammer and saw — they're *always in hand*. Likewise, the CPU never has to "fetch" R0–R15 from external memory; **every arithmetic, logic, load, or store operation happens entirely within these on-chip registers.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Performance Insight:** > The ARM Cortex-M3 uses a **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute). Because the register file is embedded inside the **Execute stage**, register-to-register ALU operations complete in **a single clock cycle**. This is why **register allocation by the compiler** is a critical performance optimization — every variable that can be held in a register avoids a costly memory round-trip. --- ### 📐 Bit-Width and Architecture Class > [!IMPORTANT] > The ARM Cortex-M3 / M4 is a **32-bit RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** architecture. > - All core registers are **32 bits (4 bytes)** wide. > - All standard data processing instructions operate on **32-bit operands** natively. > - The address bus is also **32 bits**, allowing a flat **4 GB (2³² bytes)** linear address space. $$ \text{Addressable Memory} = 2^{32} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB} $$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🏗️ 2.1 The Complete ARM Cortex-M Programmer's Model The ARM Cortex-M processor exposes a **uniform register file** to the programmer. The set is divided into two conceptual groups: --- ### 🟦 A. Low Registers (R0 – R7) - **Count:** 8 registers (R0, R1, R2, R3, R4, R5, R6, R7) - **Width:** 32 bits each - **Access:** Accessible by **all instructions** (16-bit Thumb + 32-bit Thumb-2) - **Typical Use:** - **R0 – R3:** **Argument / scratch registers** for subroutine calls (per **AAPCS — ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard**) - **R4 – R7:** **Variable registers** — may be modified freely by the callee but **must be preserved** across function calls (caller-saved semantics in AAPCS) - **Note:** Many 16-bit Thumb instructions can **only access R0–R7**, which is why they are the most frequently encoded. > [!NOTE] > **AAPCS Rule of Thumb:** *If you write assembly that modifies R4–R11, you must PUSH them onto the stack at function entry and POP them at function exit.* --- ### 🟧 B. High Registers (R8 – R12) - **Count:** 5 registers (R8, R9, R10, R11, R12) - **Width:** 32 bits each - **Access:** Only **32-bit Thumb-2 instructions** can read/write R8–R12. (16-bit Thumb instructions **cannot access** them.) - **Typical Use:** - **R12 (IP — Intra-Procedural call register):** Used by the linker as a **scratch register** between function calls (e.g., for veneers / trampolines). - **R9** has alternate roles in some implementations (e.g., **SB / TR / R9** in different ARM variants), but in **Cortex-M3/M4**, R9–R12 are fully general-purpose. - **Preservation:** R8–R11 are **callee-saved** (must be preserved). --- ### 🟥 C. Special-Purpose Core Registers (R13, R14, R15) These three registers have dedicated hardware roles in addition to their alias names: #### 🔸 R13 — SP (Stack Pointer) - **Dual-bank design in Cortex-M:** - **MSP (Main Stack Pointer):** Used by **Handler mode** (exceptions, interrupts, OS kernel) - **PSP (Process Stack Pointer):** Used by **Thread mode** (normal application code) - **Banked, not simultaneous:** At any instant, only **one of MSP or PSP is visible**. The other is dormant and switched to on exception entry/exit. - **Initial value:** Loaded from vector table offset **0x00000000** on reset (top-of-stack address). - **Stack growth direction:** ARM Cortex-M stacks grow **downward (toward lower addresses)** — *Full Descending* by AAPCS convention. $$ \text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4 \quad (\text{on each 32-bit PUSH}) $$ #### 🔸 R14 — LR (Link Register) - **Role:** Holds the **return address** when a function call (`BL` or `BLX`) is executed. - **Behavior:** - On `BL label` → `LR = PC + 4` (address of next instruction) - On function return → `POP {PC}` or `BX LR` restores execution - **Limitation:** Only one level of return address can be stored. For **nested calls**, the compiler emits a `PUSH {LR}` at function entry. $$ \text{LR} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 4 \quad \text{when BL is executed} $$ #### 🔸 R15 — PC (Program Counter) - **Role:** Points to the **next instruction to be fetched** (instruction address). - **Read Behavior:** Due to the **3-stage pipeline**, reading PC returns **current PC + 4**. - **Write Behavior:** Writing to PC causes an immediate **branch** (control transfer). - **Bit-0 must be 1:** Since all instructions in Cortex-M are Thumb (16-bit or 32-bit aligned to half-words), the **LSB (bit 0)** of any branch target must be set to **1**. The hardware automatically clears bit 0 when loading the PC. $$ \text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{current PC} + 4 $$ --- ### 🟪 D. Program Status Registers (xPSR Family) The ARM Cortex-M exposes **three PSRs**, but they are **mutually overlapping views of a single 32-bit physical register**: | View Name | Full Name | Read Bits | Write Bits | Purpose | |-----------|-----------|-----------|------------|---------| | **APSR** | Application PSR | [31:27] | [31:27] | ALU flags (N, Z, C, V, Q) | | **IPSR** | Interrupt PSR | [5:0] | None | Current exception number | | **EPSR** | Execution PSR | [26:6], [15:10] | [26:6], [15:10] | Thumb bit, ICI/IT state | $$ \text{xPSR (32-bit)} = \underbrace{\text{APSR bits}}_{\text{[31:27]}} \cup \underbrace{\text{IPSR bits}}_{\text{[5:0]}} \cup \underbrace{\text{EPSR bits}}_{\text{[26:6], [15:10]}} $$ **Bit-field Map:** | Bits | Field | Meaning | |------|-------|---------| | [31] | **N** | Negative / Less-than flag | | [30] | **Z** | Zero flag | | [29] | **C** | Carry / unsigned overflow flag | | [28] | **V** | Overflow / signed overflow flag | | [27] | **Q** | Sticky saturation flag (DSP instructions) | | [26:25] | ICI/IT | If-Then / Interrupt-Continuable Instructions state | | [24] | **T** | Thumb state — **always 1** in Cortex-M | | [15:10] | ICI/IT | Lower part of IT block state | | [5:0] | **ISR_NUMBER** | 0 = Thread mode, ≥1 = Handler number | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **T-bit (bit 24)** must always read as **1** in Cortex-M. Attempting to write a **0** to this bit triggers a **UsageFault** (HardFault escalation). This is a deliberate hardware safeguard — Cortex-M cannot execute ARM (32-bit) instructions. --- ### 🟫 E. CONTROL, PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI These are **privileged-access registers** (writable only in Handler mode or with unprivileged write disabled): | Register | Width | Function | |----------|-------|----------| | **CONTROL[0]** | 1 bit | `0` = use MSP, `1` = use PSP (in Thread mode) | | **CONTROL[1]** | 1 bit | `1` = Thread mode runs at unprivileged level | | **PRIMASK** | 1 bit | `1` = mask all exceptions except NMI & HardFault | | **FAULTMASK** | 1 bit | `1` = escalate all faults to HardFault, mask them | | **BASEPRI** | 8 bits | Mask exceptions with priority **≥ BASEPRI value** (lower priority numerically = lower urgency) | $$ \text{Exception Enabled} \iff \text{priority} < \text{BASEPRI value} $$ --- ### 📊 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | Value / Formula | Unit / Note | |---------|-----------------|-------------| | Core register count (user-visible) | **20** (R0–R15 + xPSR + 4 special) | 32-bit each | | Address space | **2³² = 4 GB** | Byte-addressable | | Stack growth direction | **Downward** (Full Descending) | FD per AAPCS | | Register read of PC | Returns **PC + 4** | Pipeline offset | | LR on `BL` | Set to **PC + 4** | Return address | | `BL` range | **±16 MB** | 32-bit Thumb-2 encoding | | Cortex-M instruction sets | **Thumb + Thumb-2** | No 32-bit ARM mode | | Banked SPs | **MSP + PSP** | Only one active | | xPSR T-bit | **Always 1** | Else → UsageFault | | AAPCS argument regs | **R0–R3** | 32-bit each | | AAPCS return register | **R0 (and R1 for 64-bit)** | — | | AAPCS callee-saved | **R4–R11** | Preserve via PUSH/POP | | AAPCS caller-saved | **R0–R3, R12** | — | | PRIMASK exception mask count | **All except NMI & HardFault** | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Bit-0 Rule for PC writes:** > The **LSB of PC** must always be **1** because Cortex-M uses Thumb instructions. The hardware silently clears bit-0 on any branch. If you `POP {PC}` from a memory location whose bit-0 is **0**, the processor will **fault**. --- ### 🌍 Engineering Real-World Utility | Domain | Use of Registers | |--------|------------------| | **RTOS Design** | PSP vs MSP selection per task; BASEPRI for critical sections | | **Bare-metal Firmware** | AAPCS compliance for C/assembly interworking | | **DSP on Cortex-M4** | APSR's Q-flag detects saturation in MAC operations | | **Interrupt Latency** | Stack-frame hardware pushing of R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR | | **Security** | CONTROL[1] enables unprivileged execution (MPU gating) | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🧮 3.1 Derivation: Stack Pointer Behavior on Exception Entry When an exception occurs, the Cortex-M3 hardware automatically performs a **stacking operation**. Let's derive the resulting SP and memory layout: **Initial conditions:** - PSP is the active SP - SP_PSP = X (top of process stack) **Step 1 — Hardware pushes 8 registers in a fixed order:** $$ \text{Stack Frame Order (decrementing addresses):} $$ $$ X - 4 \leftarrow \text{xPSR} $$ $$ X - 8 \leftarrow \text{PC} $$ $$ X - 12 \leftarrow \text{LR} $$ $$ X - 16 \leftarrow \text{R12} $$ $$ X - 20 \leftarrow \text{R3} $$ $$ X - 24 \leftarrow \text{R2} $$ $$ X - 28 \leftarrow \text{R1} $$ $$ X - 32 \leftarrow \text{R0} $$ **Step 2 — SP is decremented by 32 bytes (8 registers × 4 bytes):** $$ \text{SP}_{\text{new}} = X - 32 $$ **Step 3 — LR is loaded with a special return code (EXC_RETURN):** $$ \text{LR} = 0xFFFF\_FFF\text{x} $$ $$ \text{where bit 2 indicates:} \begin{cases} 0 \Rightarrow \text{return to MSP} \\ 1 \Rightarrow \text{return to PSP} \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 — Stack alignment requirement:** ARM AAPCS mandates **8-byte SP alignment** at public function boundaries. Hardware ensures this by inserting a **dummy 4-byte padding word** if the pre-exception SP was not 8-byte aligned — but only on **Cortex-M3 with STKALIGN bit set in CCR**, or on **Cortex-M4 / M7 always**. > [!NOTE] > **Why 8-byte alignment?** > Many ARM instructions (LDRD / STRD / VLDM) require 8-byte aligned addresses. Misalignment triggers a UsageFault (`UNALIGNED` flag in UFSR). --- ### 🧮 3.2 Derivation: APSR Flag Updates for an `ADDS` Operation For the Thumb-2 instruction `ADDS R0, R1, R2`, the flags are updated as follows: **Step 1 — Compute the sum (32-bit):** $$ S = R_1 + R_2 $$ **Step 2 — Carry flag (C):** $$ C = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } S \geq 2^{32} \text{ (unsigned overflow)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ Mathematically expressed as: $$ C = \left\lfloor \frac{R_1 + R_2}{2^{32}} \right\rfloor $$ **Step 3 — Overflow flag (V):** For **signed** (two's complement) overflow: $$ V = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if both } R_1, R_2 \text{ have same sign and } S \text{ has different sign} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ Equivalently (using bit-31 only): $$ V = \overline{(R_1^{31} \oplus R_2^{31})} \cdot (R_1^{31} \oplus S^{31}) $$ **Step 4 — Zero flag (Z):** $$ Z = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } S = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 5 — Negative flag (N):** $$ N = S^{31} $$ (Just the sign bit of the result.) > [!IMPORTANT] > **`ADCS` (Add with Carry)** uses the **C-flag as an incoming carry-in**. This is how multi-precision arithmetic (64-bit / 128-bit addition) is built from 32-bit primitives. --- ### 🧮 3.3 Derivation: AAPCS Stack Frame for a Function Call Suppose C code calls `int foo(int a, int b, int c, int d, int e)`. **Step 1 — Argument passing via registers (first 4 go in R0–R3):** $$ \text{R0} \leftarrow a, \quad \text{R1} \leftarrow b, \quad \text{R2} \leftarrow c, \quad \text{R3} \leftarrow d $$ **Step 2 — 5th argument `e` (and beyond) spilled onto the caller's stack:** $$ [\text{SP}_{\text{caller}} + 0] \leftarrow e $$ **Step 3 — Function prologue (compiler-emitted):** ``` PUSH {R4-R11, LR} ; @ Save callee-saved regs + return address SUB SP, SP, #N ; @ Allocate locals (N bytes, multiple of 8) ``` **Step 4 — Total stack bytes consumed per AAPCS:** $$ \text{Stack Bytes per call} = 8 \times 4 \text{ (R4–R11 + LR)} + N_{\text{locals}} + e_{\text{stack args}} $$ **Step 5 — Return:** $$ \text{Return value} \rightarrow \text{R0} \quad (\text{or R0:R1 pair for 64-bit}) $$ --- ### 📋 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | R0–R7 (Low) | R8–R12 (High) | R13 (SP) | R14 (LR) | R15 (PC) | |---------|-------------|---------------|----------|----------|----------| | **Bit-width** | 32 | 32 | 32 | 32 | 32 | | **General-purpose?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Stack | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ Special | | **Accessible by 16-bit Thumb?** | ✅ | ❌ | ⚠️ Some | ⚠️ Some | ⚠️ Some | | **Banked?** | No | No | ✅ MSP/PSP | No | No | | **AAPCS role** | Args / Scratch | Callee-saved | Stack mgmt | Return addr | Control flow | | **Reset value** | 0 | 0 | From vector[0] | 0xFFFFFFFF | From vector[1] | --- ### 📋 Special Register Summary Matrix | Register | Read Access | Write Access | Reset Value | Purpose | |----------|-------------|--------------|-------------|---------| | **APSR** | Privileged + User | Privileged + User | 0 | ALU flags | | **IPSR** | Privileged + User | **Read-only** | 0 | Exception number | | **EPSR** | Privileged + User | **Read-only** (mostly) | 0x01000000 (T-bit=1) | Thumb state | | **PRIMASK** | Privileged + User | Privileged only | 0 | Exception mask | | **FAULTMASK** | Privileged + User | Privileged only | 0 | Fault escalation mask | | **BASEPRI** | Privileged + User | Privileged only | 0 | Priority-based mask | | **CONTROL** | Privileged + User | Privileged only | 0 | SP select & privilege | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 🗺️ 4.1 ARM Cortex-M Programmer's Model — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CORE["ARM Cortex-M3 / M4 Core Register File (32-bit each)"] subgraph LOWREGS["Low Registers: R0-R7 (Accessible by all instructions)"] R0["R0
Argument 1 / Return value"] R1["R1
Argument 2"] R2["R2
Argument 3"] R3["R3
Argument 4"] R4["R4
Callee-saved"] R5["R5
Callee-saved"] R6["R6
Callee-saved"] R7["R7
Callee-saved"] end subgraph HIGHREGS["High Registers: R8-R12 (32-bit Thumb-2 only)"] R8["R8
Callee-saved"] R9["R9
Callee-saved"] R10["R10
Callee-saved"] R11["R11
Frame Pointer often"] R12["R12
IP Scratch register"] end subgraph SPECIAL["Special Core Registers"] R13["R13 / SP
Stack Pointer
MSP or PSP"] R14["R14 / LR
Link Register
Return Address"] R15["R15 / PC
Program Counter
Pipeline + 4 on read"] end end subgraph PSRGRP["Program Status Register (32-bit, overlapping views)"] APSR["APSR
N Z C V Q flags"] IPSR["IPSR
ISR NUMBER 5:0"] EPSR["EPSR
T-bit 24 = 1
ICI/IT bits"] end subgraph PRIVREGS["Privileged Special Registers"] PRIMASK["PRIMASK
1-bit exception mask"] FAULTMASK["FAULTMASK
1-bit fault escalator"] BASEPRI["BASEPRI
8-bit priority threshold"] CONTROL["CONTROL
SPSEL bit 0
nPRIV bit 1"] end ALU["ALU / Execute Stage
Single cycle ops"] BUS["AHB-Lite Bus Interface"] R0 --> ALU R1 --> ALU R2 --> ALU R3 --> ALU R4 --> ALU R5 --> ALU R6 --> ALU R7 --> ALU R8 --> ALU R9 --> ALU R10 --> ALU R11 --> ALU R12 --> ALU R13 --> BUS R14 --> ALU R15 --> BUS APSR --> ALU PRIMASK --> CORE FAULTMASK --> CORE BASEPRI --> CORE CONTROL --> R13 ``` --- ### 🗺️ 4.2 Stack Frame on Exception Entry — Mermaid Memory Layout ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STACK["Process Stack Memory (PSP) - grows downward"] direction TB TOP["Higher Address
Original PSP value X"] XPSR["X minus 4
xPSR saved"] PC_S["X minus 8
PC saved"] LR_S["X minus 12
LR saved"] R12_S["X minus 16
R12 saved"] R3_S["X minus 20
R3 saved"] R2_S["X minus 24
R2 saved"] R1_S["X minus 28
R1 saved"] R0_S["X minus 32
R0 saved"] NEW_SP["NEW SP = X minus 32
Hardware update"] end TOP --> XPSR --> PC_S --> LR_S --> R12_S --> R3_S --> R2_S --> R1_S --> R0_S --> NEW_SP ``` --- ### 🗺️ 4.3 AAPCS Call Convention Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR CALLER["Caller Function"] PUSHARG["Push extra args
5th onwards to stack"] BL["BL function_name
LR = PC plus 4"] CALLEE["Callee Entry"] PROLOG["PUSH R4-R11, LR
SUB SP locals"] BODY["Execute body
Use R0-R3 freely"] EPILOG["ADD SP locals
POP R4-R11, PC"] RET["Return to caller
R0 has return value"] CALLER --> PUSHARG --> BL --> CALLEE --> PROLOG --> BODY --> EPILOG --> RET --> CALLER ``` --- ### 🗺️ 4.4 APSR Flag-Update Decision Tree (for ADDS) ```mermaid flowchart TB START["ADDS R0, R1, R2"] SUM["S = R1 + R2 (32-bit)"] CARRY{"S greater than 2^32
i.e. unsigned overflow?"} N_FLAG["N = bit 31 of S"] Z_FLAG["Z = 1 if S = 0"] SIGN1{"R1 sign equals R2 sign?"} SIGN2{"S sign equals R1 sign?"} V_FLAG["V = 1 if signed overflow"] END["APSR updated
C, N, Z, V written"] START --> SUM --> CARRY CARRY -- Yes --> C_ONE["C = 1"] CARRY -- No --> C_ZERO["C = 0"] C_ONE --> N_FLAG C_ZERO --> N_FLAG N_FLAG --> Z_FLAG Z_FLAG --> SIGN1 SIGN1 -- Yes --> SIGN2 SIGN1 -- No --> V_ZERO["V = 0"] SIGN2 -- No --> V_ONE["V = 1"] SIGN2 -- Yes --> V_ZERO V_ONE --> END V_ZERO --> END ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks) --- #### ❓ Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** > *List the special-purpose core registers in the ARM Cortex-M processor and state one specific function of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Register | Full Name | Specific Function (1 mark each) | |---|----------|-----------|----------------------------------| | 1 | **R13 / SP** | Stack Pointer | Points to the **top of the active stack**; banked as **MSP (Handler)** and **PSP (Thread)** | | 2 | **R14 / LR** | Link Register | Stores the **return address (PC + 4)** when a `BL` / `BLX` subroutine call is executed | | 3 | **R15 / PC** | Program Counter | Holds the **address of the next instruction** to fetch; due to the 3-stage pipeline, a **read returns PC + 4** | --- #### ❓ Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > *Differentiate between the MSP and PSP in ARM Cortex-M3. When is each used?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | **MSP — Main Stack Pointer** | **PSP — Process Stack Pointer** | |---------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------| | Used in | **Handler mode** (exceptions, NMI, interrupts, OS kernel) | **Thread mode** (application / task code) | | Initialized by | Vector table at reset (offset 0x0) | Same vector but only used after first mode switch | | Selected via | `CONTROL[0] = 0` (default) | `CONTROL[0] = 1` | | Visibility | Active in Handler mode regardless of CONTROL | Active only in Thread mode when selected | *(1 mark each for the differentiation points + 1 mark for stating the modes of usage)* --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### ❓ Question A (14 marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Choice 1]** > **(a)** Explain the complete **ARM Cortex-M programmer's model** of registers with a neat block diagram. List the AAPCS rules for subroutine calls. (7 marks) > > **(b)** With a suitable example, explain how the **Program Status Registers (APSR, IPSR, EPSR)** are organized in ARM Cortex-M3. What is the significance of the **T-bit**? (7 marks) --- ##### ✍️ Model Solution — Part A(a) — 7 marks **The Programmer's Model (4 marks):** The ARM Cortex-M3 / M4 exposes the following register set to the programmer: 1. **R0 – R12:** Thirteen **general-purpose 32-bit registers**. - **R0 – R7 (Low registers):** accessible by **all** Thumb and Thumb-2 instructions. - **R8 – R12 (High registers):** accessible **only** by 32-bit Thumb-2 instructions. 2. **R13 / SP:** **Stack Pointer** — banked as **MSP** and **PSP**. 3. **R14 / LR:** **Link Register** — stores return addresses. 4. **R15 / PC:** **Program Counter** — points to next instruction (read returns PC + 4). 5. **xPSR:** Three overlapping views (APSR, IPSR, EPSR) of a single 32-bit status register. 6. **PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI, CONTROL:** Special mask and control registers. *(Neat block diagram carries 1 mark — refer Section 4.1 above)* **AAPCS Rules (3 marks):** | Rule | Detail | |------|--------| | **Argument passing** | First 4 word-sized arguments go in **R0, R1, R2, R3**; remaining are pushed onto the stack (right-to-left) | | **Return value** | Returned in **R0** (or **R0:R1** for 64-bit) | | **Caller-saved** | **R0 – R3, R12** — may be modified freely by the callee | | **Callee-saved** | **R4 – R11** — must be preserved by `PUSH`/`POP` in the callee | | **Stack alignment** | **8-byte aligned** at public function entries | | **Stack direction** | **Full Descending** (SP decrements on PUSH) | --- ##### ✍️ Model Solution — Part A(b) — 7 marks **PSR Organization (5 marks):** The ARM Cortex-M3 has **three PSRs** that are **three views of one 32-bit physical register**: $$ \text{xPSR} = \{\text{APSR bits}, \text{IPSR bits}, \text{EPSR bits}\} $$ | Register | Bits Used | Purpose | |----------|-----------|---------| | **APSR** | [31:27] | **N, Z, C, V, Q** flags — set by ALU ops | | **IPSR** | [5:0] | Current **exception number** (0 = Thread, 1 = Reset, …) | | **EPSR** | [26:25], [24], [15:10] | ICI/IT state, **T-bit**, ICI/IT lower | **Example — when a TIM2 interrupt fires:** - APSR still holds flags from the interrupted code - IPSR shows value **28** (assuming TIM2 IRQ# = 28) - EPSR's T-bit = 1 (Thumb mode active) **T-bit Significance (2 marks):** - The **T-bit (bit 24)** indicates the processor is in **Thumb state**. - In Cortex-M3, it **must always be 1** because the architecture only supports Thumb / Thumb-2 instructions. - An attempt to write a **0** into the T-bit triggers a **UsageFault** (which escalates to HardFault if not enabled). - This is a **security / safety feature** — it prevents accidental execution of legacy 32-bit ARM instructions on a Cortex-M core. --- #### ❓ Question B (14 marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice 2]** > **(a)** Describe the **dual-bank stack pointer** mechanism in ARM Cortex-M3. Explain how the SP is selected between MSP and PSP using the **CONTROL** register. (7 marks) > > **(b)** Explain the role of **PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, and BASEPRI** registers in exception masking. Give one example use-case for each in an RTOS. (7 marks) --- ##### ✍️ Model Solution — Question B(a) — 7 marks **Dual-Bank Stack Pointer (5 marks):** The ARM Cortex-M3 has **two physical stack pointers**: 1. **MSP — Main Stack Pointer** (default at reset) 2. **PSP — Process Stack Pointer** **Key Properties:** - Only **one SP is visible to the programmer at a time** (they are *banked*, not simultaneous). - **MSP** is used by **Handler mode** (exceptions, NMI, interrupts, OS kernel code). - **PSP** is used by **Thread mode** (application / task code). - The **active SP** is selected via the **SPSEL bit** in the CONTROL register. - The **dormant SP** retains its value across mode switches. **Selection via CONTROL (2 marks):** | CONTROL[0] | CONTROL[1] | Active SP | Privilege | |------------|------------|-----------|-----------| | 0 | 0 | **MSP** | Privileged | | 0 | 1 | **MSP** | Unprivileged | | 1 | 0 | **PSP** | Privileged | | 1 | 1 | **PSP** | Unprivileged | **Example — RTOS task switch:** ```assembly LDR R0, =task1_PSP ; Load new task's PSP MSR PSP, R0 MOVS R0, #0x02 MSR CONTROL, R0 ; SPSEL=1 → switch to PSP ``` --- ##### ✍️ Model Solution — Question B(b) — 7 marks **Exception-Masking Registers (5 marks):** | Register | Bit Width | Effect | |----------|-----------|--------| | **PRIMASK** | 1 | When set to **1**, **masks all maskable exceptions** (interrupts). Only **NMI** and **HardFault** can still fire. | | **FAULTMASK** | 1 | When set to **1**, **escalates all faults to HardFault** and masks them. Even MemManage and BusFault handlers cannot preempt. | | **BASEPRI** | 8 | Masks all exceptions whose **priority is numerically ≥ BASEPRI**. Allows **selective** interrupt masking. | **RTOS Use Cases (2 marks):** 1. **PRIMASK in Critical Section:** - Used by an RTOS to **atomically access a shared variable** without preemption. - Example: `cpsid i` instruction sets PRIMASK = 1. 2. **BASEPRI in Nested Interrupt Control:** - An RTOS sets BASEPRI to the **maximum task-level priority** so only **higher-priority (lower-numbered) interrupts** can preempt. - Example: BASEPRI = 0x40 → blocks interrupts of priority 0x40 and higher. > [!WARNING] > **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:** > > 1. **Confusing "numerically higher priority" with "higher urgency":** > In ARM, a **lower numerical value = higher urgency** (priority 0 is the highest). Writing the opposite in your answer will lose 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting that BANKED means MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE:** > Students often write "MSP and PSP can be used simultaneously." They **cannot**. Only one is visible at any moment. > > 3. **Missing the LSB-of-PC rule:** > When explaining PC writes, many students forget that **bit-0 must be 1** (Thumb alignment). This is worth at least 1 mark. > > 4. **Mixing up APSR and EPSR:** > APSR holds **flags** (writable). EPSR holds **T-bit and IT/ICI state** (mostly **read-only**). Confusing the two is a frequent error. > > 5. **Writing `LR = PC + 4` incorrectly:** > LR stores the address of the **instruction AFTER the BL**, not the BL itself. Phrase carefully: "return address", not "current PC". > > 6. **Ignoring AAPCS in assembly questions:** > Even if not asked explicitly, mentioning AAPCS compliance in any register-related answer signals examiner awareness — **bonus 1 mark** is often awarded for this. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 Quick-Fire Revision — Pin These in Memory:** > > - ✅ ARM Cortex-M3 / M4 has **20 user-visible 32-bit core registers** (R0–R15 + xPSR) plus **4 special mask/control** registers. > - ✅ **R0–R7 = Low** (all instructions); **R8–R12 = High** (Thumb-2 only). > - ✅ **R13 = SP** is **banked** as **MSP + PSP** — only one active. > - ✅ **R14 = LR** holds **PC + 4** on `BL`. > - ✅ **R15 = PC** read returns **PC + 4** (pipeline); writes cause branch. > - ✅ **Bit-0 of PC must always be 1** in Cortex-M (Thumb alignment). > - ✅ **xPSR** = APSR (flags) ∪ IPSR (exception number) ∪ EPSR (T-bit + IT/ICI). > - ✅ **T-bit always = 1** in Cortex-M; writing 0 → UsageFault. > - ✅ **CONTROL[0]** = SPSEL (MSP/PSP); **CONTROL[1]** = nPRIV (privilege level). > - ✅ **PRIMASK** = 1 masks all except NMI/HardFault. > - ✅ **FAULTMASK** = 1 escalates faults to HardFault. > - ✅ **BASEPRI** = priority threshold (lower number = higher urgency). > - ✅ **AAPCS args:** R0–R3; **return:** R0 (or R0:R1 for 64-bit); **callee-saved:** R4–R11. > - ✅ **Stack grows downward** (Full Descending) and must be **8-byte aligned** at public interfaces. > - ✅ Exception entry **auto-stacks 8 registers**: xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0. > - ✅ Cortex-M supports **Thumb + Thumb-2 only** — **no ARM 32-bit mode**.

topics.title: CPSR - Processor Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT631 - ADVANCED MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CPSR - Processor Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is a 32-bit special-purpose register in the ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) processor architecture that holds critical processor state information at any instant of execution. It is the master status register that influences every instruction the core fetches, decodes, and executes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT631 / Module 1):** The CPSR is the gateway to understanding ARM's *privilege model*, *exception handling*, and *register banking*. Mastering this register is essential for the rest of ARM programming, interrupts, and RTOS design.

The CPSR is logically divided into four distinct fields:

| Field | Bits | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Condition Flags | `[31:28]` | N, Z, C, V flags set by ALU operations |
| Status / Control | `[27:8]` | Includes I, F, T bits and reserved bits |
| Extension / GEO | `[7:6]` | Jazelle / GE flags (ARMv5+ onwards) |
| Mode Bits | `[4:0]` | Encodes the current processor mode |

### 1.2 The Seven Processor Modes of ARM

ARM architecture defines **7 operating modes**, of which **6 are privileged** (called *privileged modes*) and **1 is non-privileged** (the *User mode*). The mode is determined solely by bits **`M[4:0]`** of the CPSR.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7 Processor Modes:**
> 1. **User (USR)** — `10000` — Normal application execution, non-privileged
> 2. **FIQ (Fast IRQ)** — `10001` — High-priority interrupt handling
> 3. **IRQ (Interrupt Request)** — `10010` — Normal interrupt handling
> 4. **Supervisor (SVC)** — `10011` — Entered on reset and SWI (software interrupt)
> 5. **Abort (ABT)** — `10111` — Memory access failure (instruction/data abort)
> 6. **Undefined (UND)** — `11011` — Undefined instruction trap
> 7. **System (SYS)** — `11111` — Privileged mode using User's register set

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Processor as a Government Officer

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Officer's ID Badge":**
> Imagine the ARM processor is a **government officer** working in a large building (the SoC). The CPSR is the **ID badge hanging on his chest** that always reveals:
>
> - **Current Uniform (Mode bits `M[4:0]`)**: Is he in the General Public uniform (User), Police uniform (FIQ), or Judge's robe (Supervisor)? Each uniform gives him different powers.
> - **Mood Flags (N, Z, C, V)**: Did the last calculation make him happy (N=0, Z=1, meaning Equal) or angry (N=1)?
> - **Lockdown Orders (I and F bits)**: Has the headquarters (OS) ordered him to ignore phone calls from general public (I=1, masks IRQ) or from emergency services (F=1, masks FIQ)?
> - **Language Toggle (T bit)**: Is he currently reading the *English* manual (ARM state) or the *condensed pocket guide* (Thumb state)?
>
> Whenever an *exception* occurs (an external event, a bad memory access, an undefined instruction), the officer **automatically changes his uniform** — the mode bits in CPSR are rewritten by the hardware, and a backup of the old badge is preserved in a special register called the **Saved Program Status Register (SPSR)**.

### 1.4 Why CPSR and Modes Matter

- **Protection**: The User mode prevents application code from corrupting the OS.
- **Fast Context Switching**: Banked registers per mode mean exception handlers don't have to save the user's registers first.
- **Deterministic Interrupt Response**: The hardware-controlled mode change guarantees a clean, predictable response to asynchronous events.
- **Dual-State Execution**: The T bit enables switching between ARM (32-bit) and Thumb (16-bit) instruction sets for code density vs. performance trade-offs.

### 1.5 The Significance of `M[4:0]`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **5 mode bits** `M[4:0]` are the *only* hardware-level indicator of processor privilege. Whenever you read CPSR, bits `[4:0]` instantly tell you *which world* the processor is executing in. Writing to these bits is restricted in User mode (read-only) but allowed in any privileged mode using the `MSR` instruction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the CPSR — Bit-by-Bit Breakdown

The CPSR is a **32-bit register**, with bits allocated to specific functions. The breakdown from MSB to LSB:

| Bit(s) | Symbol | Name | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `[31]` | **N** | Negative / Less-than flag | Set when ALU result is negative (bit-31 = 1) |
| `[30]` | **Z** | Zero flag | Set when ALU result equals zero |
| `[29]` | **C** | Carry / Borrow flag | Set on unsigned overflow / no borrow |
| `[28]` | **V** | Overflow flag | Set on signed overflow |
| `[27]` | **Q** | Sticky Overflow (ARMv5+) | Set on DSP saturation, never cleared by ALU |
| `[26:25]` | — | Reserved (IT[1:0] in Thumb-2) | Jazelle / IT block state |
| `[24]` | **J** | Jazelle state (ARMv5J) | Enables Java bytecode execution |
| `[23:20]` | — | Reserved | SBZ (Should Be Zero) |
| `[19:16]` | **GE[3:0]** | Greater-Equal flags (ARMv6) | Used by SIMD instructions |
| `[15:10]` | — | Reserved | SBZ (Should Be Zero) |
| `[9]` | **E** | Endianness (ARMv6) | 0 = Little-endian, 1 = Big-endian |
| `[8]` | **A** | Imprecise Abort mask | Masks asynchronous aborts |
| `[7]` | **I** | IRQ disable | 1 = IRQ masked (disabled) |
| `[6]` | **F** | FIQ disable | 1 = FIQ masked (disabled) |
| `[5]` | **T** | Thumb state | 0 = ARM state, 1 = Thumb state |
| `[4:0]` | **M[4:0]** | **Mode bits** | Encodes current processor mode |

### 2.2 The Mode Bit Encodings — Complete Truth Table

> [!NOTE]
> The 5 mode bits `M[4:0]` follow a specific pattern: the **high 4 bits `M[4:1]`** are always set to `0b10xx` or `0b11xx`. Only specific bit patterns map to valid modes; **other encodings are UNPREDICTABLE** (no defined mode).

| Mode | M[4] | M[3] | M[2] | M[1] | M[0] | Binary | Hex | Privilege |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | `10000` | `0x10` | Non-privileged |
| **FIQ** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | `10001` | `0x11` | Privileged |
| **IRQ** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | `10010` | `0x12` | Privileged |
| **Supervisor** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | `10011` | `0x13` | Privileged |
| **Abort** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `10111` | `0x17` | Privileged |
| **Undefined** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | `11011` | `0x1B` | Privileged |
| **System** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `11111` | `0x1F` | Privileged |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Key Encodings

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Look at the **last 3 bits of M** — they form the "mode identity":
>
> - `000` → **User** (the "default citizen")
> - `001` → **FIQ** (Fastest handler, ends in 1)
> - `010` → **IRQ** (the "second-class" interrupt)
> - `011` → **Supervisor** (the "boss", entered on reset)
> - `111` → Could be **Abort, Undefined, System** (need to check upper bits)

### 2.4 Register Banking — Why Each Mode Has Its Own Registers

To enable **fast exception handling without saving user state**, ARM provides **banked registers** for each privileged mode. When the processor enters an exception mode, the banked registers are automatically available — no `PUSH` needed for them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Banked Registers per Mode:**

| Mode | Banked R8–R12 | R13 (SP) | R14 (LR) | SPSR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| User | (shared) | (shared) | (shared) | None |
| FIQ | **R8_fiq – R12_fiq** (5 extra) | R13_fiq | R14_fiq | SPSR_fiq |
| IRQ | (shared) | R13_irq | R14_irq | SPSR_irq |
| Supervisor | (shared) | R13_svc | R14_svc | SPSR_svc |
| Abort | (shared) | R13_abt | R14_abt | SPSR_abt |
| Undefined | (shared) | R13_und | R14_und | SPSR_und |
| System | (shared) | (shared) | (shared) | None |

> **Key Insight:** FIQ has **5 extra banked registers (R8_fiq–R12_fiq)**. This is intentional — it allows the FIQ handler to use these registers *without saving them*, making FIQ the **fastest possible** exception response.

### 2.5 The CPSR ↔ SPSR Relationship

The **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** is a banked copy of CPSR that exists *only* in privileged modes (one per privileged mode). The rule is:

- On **entering** an exception: hardware saves the *current* CPSR into the SPSR of the *new* mode.
- On **exiting** an exception: a `MOVS PC, LR` or `SUBS PC, LR, #4` instruction copies the SPSR back into CPSR.

> [!NOTE]
> **CPSR is the present; SPSR is the past.** Together, they form a "save-and-restore" mechanism for the processor's status across exception boundaries.

### 2.6 Mode Transition — The Automatic Flow

The ARM core does *not* enter privileged modes through normal software. It enters them through **hardware-triggered exceptions**:

| Exception Event | Mode Entered | Vector Address |
|---|---|---|
| Reset | Supervisor (SVC) | `0x00000000` |
| Undefined Instruction | Undefined (UND) | `0x00000004` |
| Software Interrupt (SWI) | Supervisor (SVC) | `0x00000008` |
| Prefetch Abort | Abort (ABT) | `0x0000000C` |
| Data Abort | Abort (ABT) | `0x00000010` |
| IRQ (normal interrupt) | IRQ | `0x00000018` |
| FIQ (fast interrupt) | FIQ | `0x0000001C` |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RTOS Design**: The CPSR's I-bit is the foundation of `OS_ENTER_CRITICAL()` and `OS_EXIT_CRITICAL()` macros.
- **Bare-Metal Bootloaders**: After reset, CPSR has `M[4:0] = 0b10011` (Supervisor) with I=1, F=1 — the OS must explicitly clear these.
- **Security/TrustZone**: The extension of CPSR into NS-bit (Non-Secure) is the cornerstone of ARM TrustZone security.
- **Debugging**: Tools like GDB read the CPSR to determine the current processor state when a breakpoint is hit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem 1 — Encoding the Mode Bits

**Question:** The CPSR of an ARM processor is read as `0x000000D3`. Identify the current processor mode.

**Step 1 — Convert CPSR to Binary**

The full CPSR value is `0x000000D3`. We only care about bits `[4:0]` (the mode bits).

$$
\text{CPSR} = 0x000000\text{D}3_{16}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the Lowest Nibble**

$$
0x\text{D}3 = 0x\text{D} \times 16^1 + 0x3 \times 16^0
$$

$$
0x\text{D}3 = 13 \times 16 + 3 = 208 + 3 = 211_{10}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the Decimal 211 to Binary**

We use the standard 8-bit binary representation. The value `211` in binary is obtained by repeated division / place-value method:

$$
211_{10} = 128 + 64 + 16 + 2 + 1
$$

$$
211_{10} = 2^7 + 2^6 + 2^4 + 2^1 + 2^0
$$

$$
211_{10} = 11010011_2
$$

**Step 4 — Extract the Lowest 5 Bits (M[4:0])**

From the 8-bit binary `11010011`, the lowest 5 bits are:

$$
M[4:0] = 10011_2
$$

**Step 5 — Match to the Mode Table**

Looking at the mode encoding table:

$$
M[4:0] = 10011_2 = 0x13_{16} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Supervisor (SVC) Mode}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify Other Bits**

We can also extract other useful information from `0xD3 = 11010011_2`:
- Bit `[7]` (I flag) = `1` → **IRQ is masked/disabled**
- Bit `[6]` (F flag) = `1` → **FIQ is masked/disabled**
- Bit `[5]` (T flag) = `0` → **ARM state (not Thumb)**

> **Final Answer:** The processor is in **Supervisor (SVC) Mode**, with both IRQ and FIQ masked, executing ARM (32-bit) instructions. This is the **typical state immediately after reset**.

---

### 3.2 Problem 2 — Setting Up a Specific Mode

**Question:** Write the value that must be written to CPSR's mode field to enter **IRQ mode with IRQs enabled and FIQs disabled**.

**Step 1 — Determine M[4:0] for IRQ Mode**

From the encoding table, IRQ mode uses:

$$
M[4:0] = 10010_2 = 0x12_{16}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the I-bit and F-bit**

- **I-bit (`bit[7]`)**: We want IRQ **enabled**, meaning it should **NOT** be masked. Therefore `I = 0`.
- **F-bit (`bit[6]`)**: We want FIQ **disabled**, meaning it **IS** masked. Therefore `F = 1`.

**Step 3 — Construct the Control Byte (Bits 7:0)**

The low byte of CPSR must contain: `M[4:0] | F<<6 | I<<7 | T<<5 | ...`

With only the relevant fields set:

$$
\text{Control Byte} = [I: F: T: M[4:0]] = [0: 1: 0: 10010]
$$

**Step 4 — Build the Binary Pattern (Bits 7 down to 0)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 7 (I)} &= 0 \\
\text{Bit 6 (F)} &= 1 \\
\text{Bit 5 (T)} &= 0 \\
\text{Bit 4 (M[4])} &= 1 \\
\text{Bit 3 (M[3])} &= 0 \\
\text{Bit 2 (M[2])} &= 0 \\
\text{Bit 1 (M[1])} &= 1 \\
\text{Bit 0 (M[0])} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Concatenate to Form the Byte**

$$
\text{Byte} = 01010010_2
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Hexadecimal**

Group the bits into nibbles: `0101 | 0010`

$$
0101_2 = 5_{16}, \quad 0010_2 = 2_{16}
$$

$$
\text{CPSR Low Byte} = 0x52
$$

**Step 7 — Construct the Full CPSR Value (Assume All Other Bits 0)**

$$
\text{CPSR} = 0x00000052
$$

**Step 8 — The MSR Instruction**

In an ARM assembly program (assuming we are already in a privileged mode like Supervisor), this is achieved by:

```assembly
MRS   R0, CPSR          @ Read current CPSR
BIC   R0, R0, #0x1F     @ Clear the 5 mode bits (bits 4:0)
ORR   R0, R0, #0x12     @ Set the new mode bits to IRQ (10010)
BIC   R0, R0, #0x80     @ Clear I-bit to ENABLE IRQ
ORR   R0, R0, #0x40     @ Set F-bit to DISABLE FIQ
MSR   CPSR_c, R0        @ Write back the control field only
```

> **Final Answer:** Write `0x52` to the low byte of CPSR, or `0x00000052` to the full CPSR. This places the processor in **IRQ mode**, with IRQs unmasked (enabled) and FIQs masked (disabled).

---

### 3.3 Problem 3 — Decoding the CPSR After a Reset

**Question:** On reset, ARM processors load CPSR with a specific value. Determine the mode, state, and interrupt mask state.

**Step 1 — Use the Standard Reset Value**

For most ARM cores, the reset value of CPSR is:

$$
\text{CPSR}_{\text{reset}} = 0x000000\text{D}3
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Low Byte to Binary**

$$
0x\text{D}3 = 11010011_2
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Each Bit Field**

| Bit | Symbol | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `[7]` | I | 1 | **IRQ Masked (disabled)** |
| `[6]` | F | 1 | **FIQ Masked (disabled)** |
| `[5]` | T | 0 | **ARM State (32-bit)** |
| `[4]` | M[4] | 1 | (part of mode) |
| `[3]` | M[3] | 0 | (part of mode) |
| `[2]` | M[2] | 0 | (part of mode) |
| `[1]` | M[1] | 1 | (part of mode) |
| `[0]` | M[0] | 1 | (part of mode) |

**Step 4 — Interpret the Mode Field**

$$
M[4:0] = 10011_2 = 0x13_{16}
$$

**Step 5 — Look Up the Mode**

From the table, `0b10011` is **Supervisor (SVC) mode**.

> **Final Answer:** After reset, the ARM core is in **Supervisor mode**, in **ARM state** (32-bit), with **both IRQ and FIQ masked**. The OS bootloader must explicitly clear the I and F bits to enable interrupts.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode vs. Banked Registers

The following matrix summarizes the **complete** register visibility per mode. Read across each row to see which banked registers that mode *owns*:

| Mode | R0–R7 | R8–R12 | R13 (SP) | R14 (LR) | R15 (PC) | SPSR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User (USR)** | Shared | Shared (USR) | Shared (USR) | Shared (USR) | Shared | None |
| **FIQ** | Shared | **Banked (R8_fiq–R12_fiq)** | **R13_fiq** | **R14_fiq** | Shared | **SPSR_fiq** |
| **IRQ** | Shared | Shared (USR) | **R13_irq** | **R14_irq** | Shared | **SPSR_irq** |
| **Supervisor (SVC)** | Shared | Shared (USR) | **R13_svc** | **R14_svc** | Shared | **SPSR_svc** |
| **Abort (ABT)** | Shared | Shared (USR) | **R13_abt** | **R14_abt** | Shared | **SPSR_abt** |
| **Undefined (UND)** | Shared | Shared (USR) | **R13_und** | **R14_und** | Shared | **SPSR_und** |
| **System (SYS)** | Shared | Shared (USR) | Shared (USR) | Shared (USR) | Shared | None |

> [!NOTE]
> **System mode is unique**: it has full **privileged** access (can write CPSR) but uses the **User's register set** (no banked copies). This makes it ideal for OS kernel code that needs to manipulate CPSR without disturbing the user's R13/R14.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Exception Vector Table

| Vector Offset | Exception | Mode Entered | CPSR After Entry (M bits) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0x00` | Reset | Supervisor | `10011` (0x13) |
| `0x04` | Undefined Instruction | Undefined | `11011` (0x1B) |
| `0x08` | Software Interrupt (SWI/SVC) | Supervisor | `10011` (0x13) |
| `0x0C` | Prefetch Abort | Abort | `10111` (0x17) |
| `0x10` | Data Abort | Abort | `10111` (0x17) |
| `0x14` | Reserved | — | — |
| `0x18` | IRQ | IRQ | `10010` (0x12) |
| `0x1C` | FIQ | FIQ | `10001` (0x11) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
